* [ABC/ESPN/ESPN2/ESPN Deportes](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN") * [CBS/Paramount+](/wiki/WNBA_on_CBS "WNBA on CBS") * [Ion](/wiki/Scripps_Sports "Scripps Sports") * [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/NBA_on_Prime_Video "NBA on Prime Video") * [NBC/Telemundo/Universo/Peacock](/wiki/WNBA_on_NBC "WNBA on NBC") (starting May 2026) * [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") (starting May 2026) * **Canada:** * [TSN](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") * [Sportsnet](/wiki/Sportsnet "Sportsnet") * [NBA TV Canada](/wiki/NBA_TV_Canada "NBA TV Canada") * **International:** * [see list](/wiki/List_of_current_WNBA_broadcasters "List of current WNBA broadcasters") |

(**WNBA**) is a women's professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") league in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") composed of 15 teams (14 in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and 1 in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")), scheduled to expand to 18 by 2030. The WNBA is one of the [professional women's sports leagues in North America](/wiki/Prominent_women%27s_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada#Professional_women's_leagues "Prominent women's sports leagues in the United States and Canada"). The WNBA is headquartered in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [WNBA](/w/index.php?title=WNBA&redirect=no "WNBA"))

Professional league in North America

"WNBA" redirects here. For other uses, see [WNBA (disambiguation)](/wiki/WNBA_(disambiguation) "WNBA (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with the [National Women's Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Basketball_Association "National Women's Basketball Association"), a 1986 league.

Women's National Basketball Association

The **Women's National Basketball Association** (**WNBA**) is a women's professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") league in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") composed of 15 teams (14 in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and 1 in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")), scheduled to expand to 18 by 2030. The WNBA is one of the [professional women's sports leagues in North America](/wiki/Prominent_women%27s_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada#Professional_women's_leagues "Prominent women's sports leagues in the United States and Canada"). The WNBA is headquartered in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The WNBA was founded on April 24, 1996, as the women's counterpart to the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA); league play began in 1997.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The regular season runs from May to September, with each team playing 44 games. The top eight teams (regardless of conference) qualify for the playoffs, culminating in the [WNBA Finals](/wiki/WNBA_Finals "WNBA Finals"), which is played in October.

The [All-Star Game](/wiki/WNBA_All-Star_Game "WNBA All-Star Game") occurs midway through the season in July. The league hosts an annual mid-season competition, the [Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "WNBA Commissioner's Cup"). The WNBA is an active member of [USA Basketball](/wiki/USA_Basketball "USA Basketball") (USAB),[[4]](#cite_note-4) which is recognized by the [International Basketball Federation](/wiki/International_Basketball_Federation "International Basketball Federation") (FIBA) as the [governing body](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") for basketball in the United States.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### League founded and play begins (1996–1997)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: League founded and play begins (1996–1997)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Sheryl_Swoopes_WNBA.jpg/250px-Sheryl_Swoopes_WNBA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheryl_Swoopes_WNBA.jpg)

[Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes"), the first player signed by the WNBA, won four championships with the [Houston Comets](/wiki/Houston_Comets "Houston Comets") and three MVP awards.

The creation of the WNBA was officially approved by the NBA Board of Governors on April 24, 1996,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and announced at a press conference with [Rebecca Lobo](/wiki/Rebecca_Lobo "Rebecca Lobo"), [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie"), and [Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes") in attendance.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The new WNBA had to compete with the recently formed [American Basketball League](/wiki/American_Basketball_League_(1996%E2%80%931998) "American Basketball League (1996–1998)"), another professional women's league that began play in the fall of 1996, but ceased operation during its 1998–99 season.

The WNBA began with eight teams: the [Charlotte Sting](/wiki/Charlotte_Sting "Charlotte Sting"), [Cleveland Rockers](/wiki/Cleveland_Rockers "Cleveland Rockers"), [Houston Comets](/wiki/Houston_Comets "Houston Comets"), and [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") in the Eastern Conference; and the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks"), [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury"), [Sacramento Monarchs](/wiki/Sacramento_Monarchs "Sacramento Monarchs"), and [Utah Starzz](/wiki/Utah_Starzz "Utah Starzz") in the Western Conference.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

While not the first major women's professional basketball league in the United States, which was the defunct [WBL](/wiki/Women%27s_Pro_Basketball_League "Women's Pro Basketball League"), the WNBA is the only league to receive full backing of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The WNBA logo, "Logo Woman", paralleled the NBA logo and was selected out of 50 different designs.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Following a much-publicized gold medal run by the USA women's national team at the [1996 Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1996 Summer Olympics"), the WNBA then began its first season on June 21, 1997. The first WNBA game featured the New York Liberty facing the Los Angeles Sparks in Los Angeles. The Liberty defeated the Sparks 67–57. A crowd of 14,284 attended the game at the [Great Western Forum](/wiki/Great_Western_Forum "Great Western Forum") in [Inglewood, California](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The game was televised nationally in the United States on the [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") television network. At the start of the 1997 season, the WNBA had television deals in with NBC (NBA rights holder), [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), and [Lifetime](/wiki/Lifetime_(TV_channel) "Lifetime (TV channel)"). [Penny Toler](/wiki/Penny_Toler "Penny Toler") scored the league's first point.[[10]](#cite_note-:12-10)

### Houston domination and league expansion (1997–2000)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Houston domination and league expansion (1997–2000)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke_in_2011.jpg/250px-Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke_in_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke_in_2011.jpg)

[Cynthia Cooper](/wiki/Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke "Cynthia Cooper-Dyke") entered the WNBA at age 34 and won Finals MVP in each of the Houston Comets’ four championships.

The WNBA centered its marketing campaign, dubbed "We Got Next," around stars [Rebecca Lobo](/wiki/Rebecca_Lobo "Rebecca Lobo"), [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie"), and [Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes").[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the league's first season, Leslie's [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks") underperformed, and Swoopes sat out much of the season due to her pregnancy. Perhaps the WNBA's first star was [MVP](/wiki/WNBA_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "WNBA Most Valuable Player Award") [Cynthia Cooper](/wiki/Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke "Cynthia Cooper-Dyke"), Swoopes' teammate on the [Houston Comets](/wiki/Houston_Comets "Houston Comets"). The Comets defeated Lobo's [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") in the [first WNBA championship game](/wiki/1997_WNBA_Championship "1997 WNBA Championship").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The initial "We Got Next" advertisement ran before each season until it was replaced with a "We Got Game" campaign.

Two teams were added in 1998 ([Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics")),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and two more in 1999 ([Orlando](/wiki/Orlando_Miracle "Orlando Miracle") and [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx")), bringing the total number of teams in the league up to 12.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The 1999 season began with a collective bargaining agreement between players and the league, marking the first [collective bargaining](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") agreement to be signed in the history of [women's professional sports](/wiki/Women%27s_professional_sports "Women's professional sports").[[15]](#cite_note-15) That year, the WNBA also announced that it would add four more teams for the 2000 season (the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever"), the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm"), the [Miami Sol](/wiki/Miami_Sol "Miami Sol"), and the [Portland Fire](/wiki/Portland_Fire_(2000%E2%80%932002) "Portland Fire (2000–2002)")), bringing the league up to 16 teams. WNBA president [Val Ackerman](/wiki/Val_Ackerman "Val Ackerman") discussed expansion by saying, "This won't be the end of it. We expect to keep growing the league."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 1999, the league's chief competition, the [American Basketball League](/wiki/American_Basketball_League_(1996%E2%80%931998) "American Basketball League (1996–1998)") (ABL), declared bankruptcy.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Many of the ABL's star players, including several Olympic gold medalists (such as [Nikki McCray](/wiki/Nikki_McCray "Nikki McCray") and [Dawn Staley](/wiki/Dawn_Staley "Dawn Staley")) and a number of standout college performers (including [Kate Starbird](/wiki/Kate_Starbird "Kate Starbird") and [Jennifer Rizzotti](/wiki/Jennifer_Rizzotti "Jennifer Rizzotti")), joined the rosters of WNBA teams, enhancing the overall quality of play in the league.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On May 23, 2000, the Comets became the first women’s professional team to be invited to the [White House Rose Garden](/wiki/White_House_Rose_Garden "White House Rose Garden").[[19]](#cite_note-:11-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) At the end of the 2000 season, the Comets won their fourth championship, capturing every title since the league's inception. Led by the "Big Three" of Cooper (who won the [Finals MVP](/wiki/WNBA_Finals_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "WNBA Finals Most Valuable Player Award") for all four championships), Swoopes, and [Tina Thompson](/wiki/Tina_Thompson "Tina Thompson"), the Comets dominated every team in the league.[[19]](#cite_note-:11-19)[[21]](#cite_note-Lieberman-2003-21) Under head coach [Van Chancellor](/wiki/Van_Chancellor "Van Chancellor"), the team posted a 98–24 record their first four seasons (16–3 in the playoffs). After 2000, Cooper retired from the league, and the Comets' dynasty came to an end.[[21]](#cite_note-Lieberman-2003-21)

### L.A. Sparks success; new league ownership and contraction (2001–2002)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: L.A. Sparks success; new league ownership and contraction (2001–2002)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/LisaLeslieDec10.jpg/250px-LisaLeslieDec10.jpg)](/wiki/File:LisaLeslieDec10.jpg)

[Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie"), a three-time MVP and the first player to dunk in a WNBA game

The Los Angeles Sparks had the best record during the [2001 WNBA season](/wiki/2001_WNBA_season "2001 WNBA season").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Led by Lisa Leslie, the Sparks posted a regular-season record of 28–4 and advanced to their first [WNBA Finals](/wiki/WNBA_Finals "WNBA Finals"), sweeping the [Charlotte Sting](/wiki/Charlotte_Sting "Charlotte Sting").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Looking to repeat in 2002, the Sparks again made a strong run toward the postseason, going 25–7 in the regular season under head coach [Michael Cooper](/wiki/Michael_Cooper "Michael Cooper"). Again, Leslie dominated her opponents throughout the Playoffs, leading the Sparks to a perfect 6–0 record, beating the New York Liberty in the [2002 Finals](/wiki/2002_WNBA_Finals "2002 WNBA Finals").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Teams and the league were collectively owned by the NBA until the end of 2002,[[25]](#cite_note-25) when the NBA sold WNBA teams either to their NBA counterparts in the same city or to a third party as a result of the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble"). This led to two teams moving: Utah moved to [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) and Orlando moved to [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun")[[27]](#cite_note-27) and became the first WNBA team to be owned by a third party instead of an NBA franchise. This sale of teams also led to two teams folding, the Miami Sol and Portland Fire, because new owners could not be found.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Bill Laimbeer leaves his mark (2003–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bill Laimbeer leaves his mark (2003–2006)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Lauren_Jackson_2a.JPG/250px-Lauren_Jackson_2a.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lauren_Jackson_2a.JPG)

[Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson"), a three-time MVP and the most accomplished international player in league history

The [Women's National Basketball Players Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Players_Association "Women's National Basketball Players Association") (WNBPA) threatened to strike in 2003 if a new deal was not worked out between players and the league. The result was a delay in the start of the 2003 preseason and the [2003 WNBA draft](/wiki/2003_WNBA_draft "2003 WNBA draft"). As a result of the strike, the league received negative publicity.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[Bill Laimbeer](/wiki/Bill_Laimbeer "Bill Laimbeer") took over the [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") in 2002 as head coach and general manager. He had high hopes for the Shock, despite the team having gone just 9–23 its previous season. Three Shock members made it to the [2003 All-Star Game](/wiki/2003_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2003 WNBA All-Star Game") ([Swin Cash](/wiki/Swin_Cash "Swin Cash"), [Cheryl Ford](/wiki/Cheryl_Ford "Cheryl Ford"), and [Deanna Nolan](/wiki/Deanna_Nolan "Deanna Nolan")) and Laimbeer orchestrated a worst-to-first turnaround with the Shock finishing the season 25–9 and in first place in the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(WNBA) "Eastern Conference (WNBA)"). In the [2003 Finals](/wiki/2003_WNBA_Finals "2003 WNBA Finals"), the Shock defeated the Los Angeles Sparks 2–1, winning game three in front of a league-record crowd of 22,076.[[32]](#cite_note-:14-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

After the [2003 season](/wiki/2003_WNBA_season "2003 WNBA season"), the [Cleveland Rockers](/wiki/Cleveland_Rockers "Cleveland Rockers"), one of the league's original eight teams, folded because its owners were unwilling to continue operating the franchise.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Bird-20180912_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bird-20180912_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bird-20180912_(cropped).jpg)

[Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird") spent her entire WNBA career with the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") and is the only player to win titles in three different decades.

The [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") had consecutive first-overall draft picks in 2001 and 2002, selecting [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson") and [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird"). The Storm's young core broke through in 2004, finishing second in the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(WNBA) "Western Conference (WNBA)") and advancing to the [Finals](/wiki/2004_WNBA_Finals "2004 WNBA Finals"). In a battle of two first-time finalists, the Storm defeated the [Connecticut Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun") 2–1 and won their first WNBA title. [Anne Donovan](/wiki/Anne_Donovan "Anne Donovan") became the first female coach to guide a team to a WNBA championship.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Val Ackerman, the first WNBA president, resigned effective February 1, 2005, citing the desire to spend more time with her family. Ackerman later became president of [USA Basketball](/wiki/USA_Basketball "USA Basketball"). On February 15, 2005, NBA commissioner [David Stern](/wiki/David_Stern "David Stern") announced that [Donna Orender](/wiki/Donna_Orender "Donna Orender"), who had been serving as the senior vice president of the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") and who had played for several teams in the now-defunct [Women's Pro Basketball League](/wiki/Women%27s_Pro_Basketball_League "Women's Pro Basketball League"), would be Ackerman's successor as of April 2005.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The WNBA awarded an expansion team to Chicago (later named the [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky")) in February 2005.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) In the off-season, a set of rule changes was approved that made the WNBA more like the NBA.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The [2005 Finals](/wiki/2005_WNBA_Finals "2005 WNBA Finals") was the first one played in a best-of-five format. In another matchup between two teams seeking their first championship, the Sun came up short for the second consecutive year, falling in four games to the [Sacramento Monarchs](/wiki/Sacramento_Monarchs "Sacramento Monarchs") led by [Yolanda Griffith](/wiki/Yolanda_Griffith "Yolanda Griffith").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2006, the league reached a milestone as the first team-oriented women's professional sports league to exist for ten consecutive seasons.[[10]](#cite_note-:12-10) On the occasion of the tenth anniversary, the WNBA released its [All-Decade Team](/wiki/WNBA%27s_All-Decade_Team "WNBA's All-Decade Team"), comprising the ten WNBA players who had contributed, through on-court play and off-court activities, the most to women's basketball during the league's existence.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The Shock bounced back in 2006 behind newly acquired [Katie Smith](/wiki/Katie_Smith "Katie Smith"), along with six remaining members from their 2003 championship run. The Shock finished second in the Eastern Conference and knocked out first-seeded Connecticut in the second round of the Playoffs. In the [Finals](/wiki/2006_WNBA_Finals "2006 WNBA Finals"), they faced reigning champion Sacramento, winning in Game Five on their home floor.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Bringing "Paul Ball" to the WNBA (2007–2009)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Bringing \"Paul Ball\" to the WNBA (2007–2009)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Diana_Taurasi_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Diana_Taurasi_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diana_Taurasi_2024_(cropped).jpg)

[Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi"), the WNBA's all-time leading scorer, spent her entire 20-year WNBA career with the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury").

In December 2006, the [Charlotte Bobcats](/wiki/Charlotte_Hornets "Charlotte Hornets") organization announced it would no longer operate the [Charlotte Sting](/wiki/Charlotte_Sting "Charlotte Sting"). Soon after, the WNBA announced that the Sting would not operate for 2007.[[44]](#cite_note-44) A [dispersal draft](/wiki/2007_WNBA_dispersal_draft "2007 WNBA dispersal draft") was held on January 8, 2007. Teams selected in inverse order of their 2006 records with the Chicago Sky receiving the first pick.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") hired coach [Paul Westhead](/wiki/Paul_Westhead "Paul Westhead") before the 2006 season. His up-tempo style of play was perfect for the team, especially after the league shortened the shot clock from 30 seconds to 24 seconds in 2006. Led by their "Big Three" of [Cappie Pondexter](/wiki/Cappie_Pondexter "Cappie Pondexter"), [Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi"), and [Penny Taylor](/wiki/Penny_Taylor "Penny Taylor"), the Mercury averaged a league-record 89.0 points per game in 2007, far surpassing the previous record, and were propelled into first place in the Western Conference.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Facing the reigning champions, the Detroit Shock, in the [2007 Finals](/wiki/2007_WNBA_Finals "2007 WNBA Finals"), the Mercury beat Detroit on their home floor in front of 22,076 fans in game five to claim their first-ever WNBA title.[[32]](#cite_note-:14-32)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In October 2007, the WNBA awarded another expansion franchise to Atlanta.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Atlanta businessman [Ron Terwilliger](/wiki/Ron_Terwilliger "Ron Terwilliger") was the original owner of the new team. Citizens of Atlanta voted on the new team's nickname and colors, selecting the name [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream").[[50]](#cite_note-DreamName-50)

During the 2008 regular season, the first-ever outdoor professional basketball game in North America, the [Liberty Outdoor Classic](/wiki/Liberty_Outdoor_Classic "Liberty Outdoor Classic"), was played at [Arthur Ashe Stadium](/wiki/Arthur_Ashe_Stadium "Arthur Ashe Stadium") in New York City. The [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") defeated the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") 71–55 in front of over 19,000 fans.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) In the [2008 Finals](/wiki/2008_WNBA_Finals "2008 WNBA Finals"), the Shock swept the first-time finalists, the [San Antonio Silver Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Silver_Stars "San Antonio Silver Stars"), winning their third championship in six years under coach Bill Laimbeer.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The victory effectively marked the end of the Shock dynasty, as Laimbeer resigned early in the 2009 season, with the team relocating shortly thereafter.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Tamika_Catchings.jpg/250px-Tamika_Catchings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tamika_Catchings.jpg)

[Tamika Catchings](/wiki/Tamika_Catchings "Tamika Catchings"), a five-time Defensive Player of the Year, spent her entire WNBA career with the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever").

Late in 2008, the WNBA took over ownership of one of the league's original franchises, the Houston Comets. The Comets ceased operations on December 1, 2008, after no owners for the franchise could be found.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) A [dispersal draft](/wiki/2008_WNBA_dispersal_draft "2008 WNBA dispersal draft") took place on December 8, 2008, with the first pick, [Sancho Lyttle](/wiki/Sancho_Lyttle "Sancho Lyttle"), taken by the Atlanta Dream.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Westhead left the Mercury following the 2007 title, and the team faltered in 2008, becoming the first defending champion in league history to miss the playoffs. New head coach [Corey Gaines](/wiki/Corey_Gaines "Corey Gaines") implemented Westhead's style of play, and in the 2009, the Mercury once again broke their own record, averaging 92.8 points per game in the regular season. Helped by the return of Taylor, who missed the 2008 season and a large part of the 2009 season, the Mercury clinched first place in the Western Conference and advanced to the [2009 Finals](/wiki/2009_WNBA_Finals "2009 WNBA Finals").[[59]](#cite_note-59) The championship series was a battle of contrasting styles as the Mercury, number one league offense, had to face the Indiana Fever, number three league defense. Phoenix won Game One in overtime, 120–116, the highest scoring game in WNBA playoffs history, and defeated the Fever in five games, capturing their second WNBA championship.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

Not only did Paul Westhead's system influence his Mercury team, but it created a domino effect throughout the league. Young athletic players were capable of scoring more and playing at a faster pace. As a league, the 2010 average of 80.35 points per game was the best, far surpassing the 69.2 average in the league's [inaugural season](/wiki/1997_WNBA_season "1997 WNBA season").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Changing of the guard (2010–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Changing of the guard (2010–2012)")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Augustus_20171004.jpg/250px-Augustus_20171004.jpg)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Maya_Moore_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Maya_Moore_%28cropped%29.jpg)

[Seimone Augustus](/wiki/Seimone_Augustus "Seimone Augustus") and [Maya Moore](/wiki/Maya_Moore "Maya Moore"), two cornerstones of the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") dynasty that won four titles between 2011 and 2017.

On October 20, 2009, the WNBA announced that the [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") would relocate to [Tulsa, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tulsa,_Oklahoma "Tulsa, Oklahoma"), to become the [Tulsa Shock](/wiki/Tulsa_Shock "Tulsa Shock").[[63]](#cite_note-63) On November 20, 2009, the WNBA announced that the [Sacramento Monarchs](/wiki/Sacramento_Monarchs "Sacramento Monarchs") had folded due to lack of support from its current owners, the [Maloof family](/wiki/Maloof_family "Maloof family"), who were also the owners of the [Sacramento Kings](/wiki/Sacramento_Kings "Sacramento Kings") at the time.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The league announced it would seek new owners to relocate the team to the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"); however, no ownership was found and a [dispersal draft](/wiki/2009_WNBA_dispersal_draft "2009 WNBA dispersal draft") was held on December 14, 2009.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) The folding of the Monarchs left the WNBA with twelve teams, a number that remained unchanged for the next fifteen seasons.

The 2010 season saw a tight race in the [East](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(WNBA) "Eastern Conference (WNBA)"), which held a .681 winning percentage over the [West](/wiki/Western_Conference_(WNBA) "Western Conference (WNBA)"), its highest ever. However, it was a Western team, the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm"), who ultimately dominated the league. Led once again by the duo of [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird") and [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson"), the only players remaining from the 2004 championship team, the Storm finished 28–6 in the regular season and a perfect 7–0 in the playoffs.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Although the Storm swept the [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream"), the [2010 Finals](/wiki/2010_WNBA_Finals "2010 WNBA Finals") were a close affair, with Seattle winning the three games by a combined eight points.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The Dream, who had finished their inaugural 2008 season 4–30, made a quick turnaround behind the 2009 first-overall pick [Angel McCoughtry](/wiki/Angel_McCoughtry "Angel McCoughtry") and advanced to the Finals in only their third year.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

After the 2010 season, President Orender announced she would be resigning from her position as of December 31.[[71]](#cite_note-71) On April 21, 2011, NBA commissioner [David Stern](/wiki/David_Stern "David Stern") announced that former [Girl Scouts of the USA](/wiki/Girl_Scouts_of_the_USA "Girl Scouts of the USA") Senior Vice President and Chief Marketing Officer, [Laurel J. Richie](/wiki/Laurel_J._Richie "Laurel J. Richie"), would assume duties as president on May 16, 2011.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In their first eleven seasons in the league, the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") saw little success, winning only one playoff game in total. Before the 2010 season, [Cheryl Reeve](/wiki/Cheryl_Reeve "Cheryl Reeve") took over as head coach, and the team added [Rebekkah Brunson](/wiki/Rebekkah_Brunson "Rebekkah Brunson") and [Lindsay Whalen](/wiki/Lindsay_Whalen "Lindsay Whalen") to support 2006 first-overall pick [Seimone Augustus](/wiki/Seimone_Augustus "Seimone Augustus"). The Lynx failed to make the playoffs once again in Reeve's first season, but were able to select [Maya Moore](/wiki/Maya_Moore "Maya Moore") with the first pick in the 2011 draft. The core of Augustus, Brunson, Moore, and Whalen would go on to make six Finals appearances and win four championships over the next seven years. Their first title came immediately in 2011, with the Lynx dominating the regular season and dropping only one game throughout their playoff run.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In the [2011 Finals](/wiki/2011_WNBA_Finals "2011 WNBA Finals"), the Dream were swept for the second straight year despite more heroics from McCoughtry, who set still-standing league records for most points in a playoff game (42) and in a Finals game (38) during the Dream's consecutive Finals appearances.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

The Lynx made it back to the [2012 Finals](/wiki/2012_WNBA_Finals "2012 WNBA Finals") but fell in four games to the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever"), led by [Tamika Catchings](/wiki/Tamika_Catchings "Tamika Catchings"). In what was far from a smooth ride, the Fever had to stave off elimination in both of the first two rounds and lost their second-leading scorer, [Katie Douglas](/wiki/Katie_Douglas_(basketball) "Katie Douglas (basketball)"), to injury along the way before ultimately capturing their first WNBA championship.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### The "Three to See" (2013–2019)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The \"Three to See\" (2013–2019)")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Brittney_Griner_3_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-Brittney_Griner_3_%28cropped2%29.jpg)

[Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/20140814_Elena_Delle_Donne_2.jpg/250px-20140814_Elena_Delle_Donne_2.jpg)

[Elena Delle Donne](/wiki/Elena_Delle_Donne "Elena Delle Donne")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Skylar_Diggins-Smith_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Skylar_Diggins-Smith_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg)

[Skylar Diggins](/wiki/Skylar_Diggins "Skylar Diggins")

The top three picks of the [2013 WNBA draft](/wiki/2013_WNBA_draft "2013 WNBA draft"), labeled "The Three To See".

The much-publicized [2013 WNBA draft](/wiki/2013_WNBA_draft "2013 WNBA draft") produced [Baylor](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_women%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears women's basketball") center [Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_Fightin%27_Blue_Hens_women%27s_basketball "Delaware Fightin' Blue Hens women's basketball") forward [Elena Delle Donne](/wiki/Elena_Delle_Donne "Elena Delle Donne"), and [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_women%27s_basketball "Notre Dame Fighting Irish women's basketball") guard [Skylar Diggins](/wiki/Skylar_Diggins "Skylar Diggins") as the top three picks. It was the first WNBA draft televised in prime time on ESPN after the league extended its TV deal with the network through 2022.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Griner, Delle Donne, and Diggins were thus labeled "The Three To See".[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) The retirement of legends [Ticha Penicheiro](/wiki/Ticha_Penicheiro "Ticha Penicheiro"), [Katie Smith](/wiki/Katie_Smith "Katie Smith"), [Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes"), and [Tina Thompson](/wiki/Tina_Thompson "Tina Thompson")[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) coupled with the arrival of highly touted rookies and new rule changes effectively marked the end of an era for the WNBA and the ushering of another.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The promotion of "The Three To See" helped boost television ratings for the league by 28 percent, and half of the teams ended the 2013 season profitable.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) The improved health of the league was on display after the season, when the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks")' ownership group folded; it took the league only a few weeks to line up [Guggenheim Partners](/wiki/Guggenheim_Partners "Guggenheim Partners") to purchase the team, and the franchise also garnered interest from the ownership of the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors").[[85]](#cite_note-85) On the court, the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") won their second title in three years, sweeping the [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream") in the [2013 Finals](/wiki/2013_WNBA_Finals "2013 WNBA Finals").[[86]](#cite_note-86)

This narrative was reinforced by the [2014 Finals](/wiki/2014_WNBA_Finals "2014 WNBA Finals"), which featured a matchup between the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") (led by Griner and [Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi")) and the [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky") (led by Delle Donne and [Sylvia Fowles](/wiki/Sylvia_Fowles "Sylvia Fowles")) and became the most-watched series since 2003.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The Mercury swept the first-time finalist Sky to cap a dominant season: they went 29–5 in the regular season, the best winning percentage since the 2001 Sparks.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Sylvia_Fowles_2022_%2852283271158%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sylvia_Fowles_2022_%2852283271158%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sylvia_Fowles_2022_(52283271158)_(cropped).jpg)

[Sylvia Fowles](/wiki/Sylvia_Fowles "Sylvia Fowles"), a two-time Finals MVP and four-time Defensive Player of the Year

However, the 2015 season began without several stars of the previous Finals. Taurasi was paid by her Russian club, where she played during the WNBA offseason, to sit out the season,[[89]](#cite_note-89) while Fowles refused to play for Chicago before she was traded to the Lynx in July.[[90]](#cite_note-90) That proved enough to swing the balance, as the Lynx returned to the top by defeating the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") in five games in the [2015 Finals](/wiki/2015_WNBA_Finals "2015 WNBA Finals"). Fowles was named Finals MVP, helping the Lynx avenge their 2012 defeat.[[91]](#cite_note-91) After the 2015 season, the [Tulsa Shock](/wiki/Tulsa_Shock "Tulsa Shock") moved to the [Dallas–Fort Worth](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth "Dallas–Fort Worth") region and were renamed the [Dallas Wings](/wiki/Dallas_Wings "Dallas Wings").[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) The league also saw another change in leadership, as president [Laurel J. Richie](/wiki/Laurel_J._Richie "Laurel J. Richie") left her position in November 2015[[94]](#cite_note-94) and was succeeded by [Lisa Borders](/wiki/Lisa_Borders "Lisa Borders") in February 2016.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Beginning in 2016, the league abolished conference-based playoff seeding: the top eight teams by record, regardless of conference, advanced to the postseason, with the top two regular-season teams receiving byes directly to the semifinals.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Coincidentally, the next two seasons were marked by a clear separation between two Western teams, the Lynx and the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks"), and the rest of the league, culminating in back-to-back five-game Finals matchups.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) In the [2016 Finals](/wiki/2016_WNBA_Finals "2016 WNBA Finals"), the Sparks, led by [Candace Parker](/wiki/Candace_Parker "Candace Parker") and [Nneka Ogwumike](/wiki/Nneka_Ogwumike "Nneka Ogwumike"), won their first championship since 2002, taking Game Five in Minnesota on a game-winning Ogwumike basket with 3.1 seconds left.[[99]](#cite_note-99) The Lynx earned their revenge in the [2017 Finals](/wiki/2017_WNBA_Finals "2017 WNBA Finals"), defeating the Sparks in five games.[[100]](#cite_note-100) It marked Minnesota’s fourth title in seven years and the final chapter of the franchise’s dynasty, as after an unsuccessful 2018 season, Brunson, Moore, and Whalen all effectively retired from the league.

Following the [2017 season](/wiki/2017_WNBA_season "2017 WNBA season") the [San Antonio Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars") moved to Nevada, becoming the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces").[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) In October 2018, Borders stepped down as president.[[103]](#cite_note-103) [Mark Tatum](/wiki/Mark_Tatum "Mark Tatum") served as interim president until May 2019, when [Cathy Engelbert](/wiki/Cathy_Engelbert "Cathy Engelbert") was named WNBA commissioner, a role that replaced the league’s president title.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Breanna_Stewart_WNBA_Finals_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Breanna_Stewart_WNBA_Finals_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Breanna_Stewart_WNBA_Finals_2024_(cropped).jpg)

[Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart"), a two-time MVP and three-time champion with the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") and [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty")

After their 2010 championship, the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") posted only one winning season in the next seven years. However, that put them in position to draft back-to-back No. 1 overall picks for the second time in franchise history: [Jewell Loyd](/wiki/Jewell_Loyd "Jewell Loyd") in 2015 and [Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart") in 2016. Alongside the ageless Sue Bird, they returned the Storm to the top in 2018. Their toughest playoff test came in a five-game semifinal series against the Mercury.[[105]](#cite_note-105) In the [Finals](/wiki/2018_WNBA_Finals "2018 WNBA Finals"), Seattle swept the first-time finalist [Washington Mystics](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics"), led by Delle Donne, who had been traded to Washington a year earlier.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

With Stewart and Bird missing the 2019 season due to injuries, the Mystics dominated the regular season behind Delle Donne's MVP campaign and the first [50-40-90 season](/wiki/50-40-90_season "50-40-90 season") in WNBA history. Led by [Mike Thibault](/wiki/Mike_Thibault "Mike Thibault"), the winningest coach in WNBA history, the Mystics ran the best offense in league history,[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) and returned to the [Finals](/wiki/2019_WNBA_Finals "2019 WNBA Finals") for a second straight year. There, they faced the [Connecticut Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun"), who reached the Finals for the first time since 2005—coincidentally, then coached by Thibault. The Mystics prevailed in five games to win the first championship in franchise history, as well as the first for Delle Donne and Thibault.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

### New collective bargaining agreement and expansion (2020–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New collective bargaining agreement and expansion (2020–2023)")]

During the 2018 season, the [WNBA players' union](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Players_Association "Women's National Basketball Players Association") opted out of the collective bargaining agreement (CBA) with the league, which ended after the 2019 season. In January 2020, the league and union announced that they had reached an agreement on a new CBA to take effect with the 2020 season and running through 2027, with an opt-out after 2025. The agreement increased player compensation by more than 50% and expanded benefits, including fully paid maternity leave. It also broadened free‑agency rights. In addition, the CBA introduced a “prioritization” clause that penalizes veteran players who report late to WNBA training camp after returning from overseas play.[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

Also in January 2020, the WNBA announced a new in-season tournament, the [Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "WNBA Commissioner's Cup"), originally slated to debut in the [2020 season](/wiki/2020_WNBA_season "2020 WNBA season").[[113]](#cite_note-113) On April 3, the WNBA announced that due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the beginning of the 2020 season would be postponed. The [2020 entry draft](/wiki/2020_WNBA_draft "2020 WNBA draft") took place as originally scheduled on April 17, although it was done remotely.[[114]](#cite_note-114) In June, WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert announced plans for a 22‑game regular season beginning in July, with a traditional playoff format, to be held exclusively at [IMG Academy](/wiki/IMG_Academy "IMG Academy") in [Bradenton, Florida](/wiki/Bradenton,_Florida "Bradenton, Florida"). The players were housed at the Bradenton complex, and all games and practices took place there.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Several players opted out for medical or personal reasons.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The reigning champion Mystics were particularly affected, Delle Donne among those absent, and barely made the playoffs. The shortened regular season was largely dominated by the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") and the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces"), who ultimately met in the [Finals](/wiki/2020_WNBA_Finals "2020 WNBA Finals"). It was the Aces franchise's first Finals appearance since 2008, and their first since relocating to Las Vegas. The Storm, back at full strength, swept their playoff run to win their second title in three years.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

On March 15, 2021, an announcement was made that the WNBA would introduce a ceremonial logo, basketball, and uniforms as part of its 25th anniversary celebratory campaign called "Count It".[[118]](#cite_note-118) As part of the campaign, the league unveiled [The W25](/wiki/The_W25 "The W25"), a list of 25 players determined to be the league's greatest and most influential, as chosen by a panel of media and pioneering women's players.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Candace_Parker_Chicago_Sky_2021_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Candace_Parker_Chicago_Sky_2021_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Candace_Parker_Chicago_Sky_2021_(cropped).jpg)

[Candace Parker](/wiki/Candace_Parker "Candace Parker"), the only player to win the MVP award as a rookie and the only player to win championships with three different teams.

The [2021 season](/wiki/2021_WNBA_season "2021 WNBA season") largely returned to normal conditions and featured a 32‑game regular season with a month‑long Olympic break for the postponed [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").[[120]](#cite_note-120) The Commissioner's Cup officially debuted that year.[[121]](#cite_note-121) The [inaugural Cup Final](/wiki/2021_WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "2021 WNBA Commissioner's Cup"), held on August 12 as the first game after the Olympic break, was streamed via [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"), and the reigning WNBA champions, the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm"), won the first edition by defeating the [Connecticut Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun").[[122]](#cite_note-122) But neither team replicated its success in the playoffs, and in a season full of twists, the [2021 WNBA Finals](/wiki/2021_WNBA_Finals "2021 WNBA Finals") produced a rematch of the 2014 Finals between the fifth-seeded [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") and the sixth-seeded [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky"). Benefiting from expanded free‑agency rights under the new CBA, the Sky were able to sign [Candace Parker](/wiki/Candace_Parker "Candace Parker") before the season to bolster their established core.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Chicago prevailed in four games to win the first championship in franchise history.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In February 2022, the league raised $75 million in capital, on terms valuing the league at $475 million. Under the deal, investors received 16 percent of the league's equity. In mid-2022, commissioner Cathy Engelbert confirmed that the league was actively considering expansion.[[125]](#cite_note-:2-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/A%27ja_Wilson_%2853756794398%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-A%27ja_Wilson_%2853756794398%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A%27ja_Wilson_(53756794398)_(cropped).jpg)

[A'ja Wilson](/wiki/A%27ja_Wilson "A'ja Wilson"), a four-time MVP and three-time champion with the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces")

Before the 2022 season, the Las Vegas Aces signed head coach [Becky Hammon](/wiki/Becky_Hammon "Becky Hammon") to a then‑unprecedented $1 million annual salary.[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) The move was the latest in a series of ambitious investments by the team’s new owner, [Mark Davis](/wiki/Mark_Davis_(American_football) "Mark Davis (American football)"), who purchased the franchise in February 2021,[[128]](#cite_note-128) including the construction of a $40 million dedicated training facility, the first of its kind in the league.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) The Aces' core included consecutive first-overall picks from 2017 to 2019—[Kelsey Plum](/wiki/Kelsey_Plum "Kelsey Plum"), [A'ja Wilson](/wiki/A%27ja_Wilson "A'ja Wilson"), and [Jackie Young](/wiki/Jackie_Young "Jackie Young")—along with [Chelsea Gray](/wiki/Chelsea_Gray "Chelsea Gray"), whom they signed in free agency in 2021. In 2022, with the regular season expanded to 36 games, Las Vegas finished with the best regular‑season record and won the [Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/2022_WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "2022 WNBA Commissioner's Cup"). That season, the playoff format reverted to a traditional bracket without byes for top seeds and with a best‑of‑three first round. The Aces advanced to the [Finals](/wiki/2022_WNBA_Finals "2022 WNBA Finals") and defeated the Connecticut Sun in four games to win the first championship in franchise history.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

The 2023 season was widely framed as a battle between two "superteams": the Aces and the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty"). The Aces added Candace Parker in free agency, while the Liberty signed free agents Breanna Stewart and [Courtney Vandersloot](/wiki/Courtney_Vandersloot "Courtney Vandersloot") and traded for [Jonquel Jones](/wiki/Jonquel_Jones "Jonquel Jones"), joining 2020 first-overall pick [Sabrina Ionescu](/wiki/Sabrina_Ionescu "Sabrina Ionescu") in New York.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The two franchises were frequently cited as examples of increased owner investment, and both drew league scrutiny, resulting in investigations or penalties, for providing player benefits beyond those stipulated by the CBA.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) That year, the league expanded the regular‑season schedule to 40 games. The season largely matched expectations: the two teams met in the [Commissioner's Cup Final](/wiki/2023_WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "2023 WNBA Commissioner's Cup"), which the Liberty won, and then again in the [WNBA Finals](/wiki/2023_WNBA_Finals "2023 WNBA Finals").[[136]](#cite_note-136) Despite playing without Parker throughout the playoffs and losing two starters to injury during the Finals, the Aces defeated the Liberty in four games and became the first repeat champions since the 2001–02 [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks").[[137]](#cite_note-137) The regular season was the most-watched in 21 years and the Finals were the most-watched since 2003.[[138]](#cite_note-138)[[139]](#cite_note-139)

On October 5, 2023, the WNBA announced an agreement with the owners of the NBA's [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") to bring a team to [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), with a reported $50 million expansion fee payable over ten years.[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141) The [Golden State Valkyries](/wiki/Golden_State_Valkyries "Golden State Valkyries") became the league's first expansion team since the [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream") in 2008 and joined as the thirteenth team in 2025.[[142]](#cite_note-142) On May 23, 2024, the league announced that the Kilmer Group had acquired a franchise based in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") that will debut in 2026.[[143]](#cite_note-143) On September 18, 2024, the league announced that the Bhathal family, owners of the [Portland Thorns](/wiki/Portland_Thorns "Portland Thorns") of the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") (NWSL), had acquired a franchise based in [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") that will also debut in 2026.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)

### Popularity surge and further expansion plans (2024–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Popularity surge and further expansion plans (2024–present)")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Caitlin_Clark_Fever_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Caitlin_Clark_Fever_%28cropped%29.jpg)

[Caitlin Clark](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark "Caitlin Clark")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Angel_Reese_September_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Angel_Reese_September_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)

[Angel Reese](/wiki/Angel_Reese "Angel Reese")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Paige_Bueckers_2025_4_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Paige_Bueckers_2025_4_%28cropped%29.jpg)

[Paige Bueckers](/wiki/Paige_Bueckers "Paige Bueckers")

Clark, Reese, and Bueckers were widely viewed as three of the leading new faces of the 2024–25 popularity surge.

The [2024 draft](/wiki/2024_WNBA_draft "2024 WNBA draft") drew unprecedented attention amid surging interest and record viewership in women's [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball"), particularly the [2023](/wiki/2023_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_championship_game "2023 NCAA Division I women's basketball championship game") and [2024 NCAA championship games](/wiki/2024_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_championship_game "2024 NCAA Division I women's basketball championship game"). The draft class was led by first-overall pick [Caitlin Clark](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark "Caitlin Clark"), widely regarded as [the main driver of the surge in attention](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark_effect "Caitlin Clark effect"), and featured other highly followed players, including [Angel Reese](/wiki/Angel_Reese "Angel Reese"). The draft averaged 2.45 million viewers, more than four times the previous record for a WNBA draft.[[146]](#cite_note-146) It was the first of numerous viewership records set during the record-setting [2024 season](/wiki/2024_WNBA_season "2024 WNBA season"), the majority of which involved Clark’s [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever"); games featuring both [Clark and Reese](/wiki/Clark%E2%80%93Reese_rivalry "Clark–Reese rivalry") proved a particular draw.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148) The [2024 All-Star Game](/wiki/2024_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2024 WNBA All-Star Game") averaged 3.44 million viewers, delivering the league’s largest audience since its opening weekend in 1997.[[149]](#cite_note-149) Attendance also rose, with the league and individual teams setting multiple highs, including a regular‑season record of 20,711 for a Mystics–Fever game in Washington.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) In July 2024, the league announced a new 11-year media deal worth $2.2 billion.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

In 2024, the reigning champions, the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces"), faltered despite a historic season by [A'ja Wilson](/wiki/A%27ja_Wilson "A'ja Wilson").[[153]](#cite_note-153) The [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") and the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") squared off in both the [Commissioner's Cup Final](/wiki/2024_WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "2024 WNBA Commissioner's Cup"), which the Lynx won, and the [WNBA Finals](/wiki/2024_WNBA_Finals "2024 WNBA Finals"). The five-game Finals were one of the closest in history, with two games going to overtime and two others decided by a single possession. For the first time in WNBA Finals history, a decisive Game Five went to overtime. The Liberty ultimately prevailed, after a controversial ending,[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156) to win the first championship in franchise history.[[157]](#cite_note-157) The playoffs were no exception to the rising interest, and the Finals drew their largest audience in 25 years.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

With the [Golden State Valkyries](/wiki/Golden_State_Valkyries "Golden State Valkyries") joining the league, the [2025 season](/wiki/2025_WNBA_season "2025 WNBA season") expanded to 44 games. Despite Clark and other stars missing significant time due to injuries, the league largely sustained upward trends in interest, viewership, and attendance,[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) highlighted by the Valkyries immediately setting attendance records.[[162]](#cite_note-162) Investment continued to rise across the league, and by the end of 2025 more than half of franchises had either opened or announced dedicated training facilities.[[163]](#cite_note-163) On August 15, 2025, the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") and the [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream") played the first-ever international WNBA regular-season game at [Rogers Arena](/wiki/Rogers_Arena "Rogers Arena") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver").[[164]](#cite_note-164) The Lynx topped the regular‑season standings, but were upset by the Fever in the [Commissioner's Cup Final](/wiki/2025_WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "2025 WNBA Commissioner's Cup") and by the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") in the playoffs. In the [WNBA Finals](/wiki/2025_WNBA_Finals "2025 WNBA Finals"), played in a best-of-seven format for the first time, the Mercury faced the Aces. Las Vegas struggled midseason but closed the regular season on a 16-game win streak,[[165]](#cite_note-165) with Wilson earning a record fourth MVP.[[166]](#cite_note-166) The Aces swept the Mercury to claim their third title in four years.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

On June 30, 2025, the WNBA announced expansion teams in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The Cleveland team is scheduled to begin play in 2028, followed by Detroit in 2029, and Philadelphia in 2030.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

#### 2025 CBA negotiations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2025 CBA negotiations")]

In July 2025, during the [WNBA All-Star Weekend](/wiki/2025_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2025 WNBA All-Star Game"), the [WNBA players union](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Players_Association "Women's National Basketball Players Association") and the league officials had a meeting regarding a new collective bargaining agreement (CBA).[[169]](#cite_note-:10-169) The current CBA, which was signed in 2020, was set to expire in 2027, but in October 2024 the WNBPA exercised its right to opt out of the agreement, effective on October 31, 2025.[[170]](#cite_note-170) More than 40 players were present at the meeting, which they described as a "missed opportunity",[[169]](#cite_note-:10-169) as both sides did not get any closer to a new deal. Meanwhile, [Engelbert](/wiki/Cathy_Engelbert "Cathy Engelbert") painted the conversation as "very constructive".[[171]](#cite_note-171) Players have said that revenue sharing, salary structure, and prioritization are some of the key issues that they do not agree on.[[172]](#cite_note-172) On July 19, before the All-Star Game, all players wore "Pay Us What You Owe Us" T-shirts during warmups and fans chanted "pay them!" in support after the game.[[173]](#cite_note-173) In August, representatives of the WNBA and the WNBPA had another meeting in New York to continue the negotiations.[[174]](#cite_note-174) On October 30, the WNBA and the WNBPA agreed to an extension, pushing the deadline for the CBA expiration for 30 days, until November 30.[[175]](#cite_note-175) On November 30, the WNBA and the WNBPA agreed to a second extension, pushing expiration deadline until January 9, 2026.[[176]](#cite_note-176) On December 18, the WNBPA announced that the union had authorized the executive committee to "call a strike when necessary" as players continued negotiations with the league over a new CBA.[[177]](#cite_note-177) If both sides don't reach an agreement, the league could experience a [lockout](/wiki/Lockout_(industry) "Lockout (industry)"), which has never happened in its history.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

*Future teams*

The WNBA originated with eight teams in 1997, and through a sequence of expansions, contractions, and relocations will consist of 15 teams as of the [2026 season](/wiki/2026_WNBA_season "2026 WNBA season").

As of the 2026 season, the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces") (formerly known as the [Utah Starzz](/wiki/Utah_Starzz "Utah Starzz") and [San Antonio (Silver) Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars")), [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks"), [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty"), and [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") are the only remaining franchises that were founded in 1997.

Arenas listed below reflect those in use during the 2026 season.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-180)** The Tempo has announced that during its inaugural 2026 season fifteen home games will be played at [Coca-Cola Coliseum](/wiki/Coca-Cola_Coliseum "Coca-Cola Coliseum") and three at [Scotiabank Arena](/wiki/Scotiabank_Arena "Scotiabank Arena"), both located in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), two at [Bell Centre](/wiki/Bell_Centre "Bell Centre") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), and two at [Rogers Arena](/wiki/Rogers_Arena "Rogers Arena") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver").[[179]](#cite_note-179)
