### Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Timeline")]

Main article: [Timeline of the WNBA](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_WNBA "Timeline of the WNBA")

### Relationship with NBA teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Relationship with NBA teams")]

#### Share ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Share ownership")]

WNBA teams affiliated with an [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") team from the same market are known as sister teams. These include the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") and [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"), the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") and [Pacers](/wiki/Indiana_Pacers "Indiana Pacers"), the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") and [Timberwolves](/wiki/Minnesota_Timberwolves "Minnesota Timberwolves"), the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") and [Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns"), the [Washington Mystics](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics") and [Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards"), the [Golden State Valkyries](/wiki/Golden_State_Valkyries "Golden State Valkyries") and [Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors"), and future expansion teams the [Toronto Tempo](/wiki/Toronto_Tempo "Toronto Tempo") (2026) and [Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland_WNBA_team "Cleveland WNBA team") (2028) and the [Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_WNBA_team "Detroit WNBA team") (2029) and the [Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons"), and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_WNBA_team "Philadelphia WNBA team") (2030) and the [76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers"). Of these teams, only the Mystics and Tempo don't share an arena with their NBA counterpart.

The Liberty were previously associated with the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks"), having been owned by the Knicks' parent company, the [Madison Square Garden Company](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden_Company "Madison Square Garden Company"),[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182) but the team was sold in January 2019 to a group led by [Joseph Tsai](/wiki/Joseph_Tsai "Joseph Tsai"), then a minority owner of the Brooklyn Nets[[182]](#cite_note-183) and now sole owner of that team.[[183]](#cite_note-184) The Liberty, which played at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") until 2017 and at [Westchester County Center](/wiki/Westchester_County_Center "Westchester County Center") in suburban White Plains in 2018 and 2019, were then relocated to the Nets' home arena, [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"), in downtown Brooklyn.

#### Share market with an NBA team, but not ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Share market with an NBA team, but not ownership")]

Amongst the WNBA teams that are in the same market as an NBA team, but are independently owned, are the [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky"), who are not affiliated with the [Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") and play in a separate arena from their NBA counterpart.

The [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") was the sister team of the [Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons") until the teams' owner sold the Shock to investors who [moved the team](/wiki/Tulsa_Shock "Tulsa Shock") to [Tulsa, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tulsa,_Oklahoma "Tulsa, Oklahoma"). The franchise relocated again in 2016, this time to the [Dallas–Fort Worth metro area](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex") to become the [Dallas Wings](/wiki/Dallas_Wings "Dallas Wings"). However, the Wings are not affiliated with the [Mavericks](/wiki/Dallas_Mavericks "Dallas Mavericks") and also play in a separate arena.

While the [Atlanta Dream](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream") shared [State Farm Arena](/wiki/State_Farm_Arena "State Farm Arena") with the [Hawks](/wiki/Atlanta_Hawks "Atlanta Hawks") from 2008 to 2016 and again in 2019, the Hawks never held any ownership stake in the WNBA team. The [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks") share an arena with the [Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), but not ownership. However, Lakers' legend [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson") has an ownership stake in the Sparks.

The future [Portland Fire](/wiki/Portland_Fire "Portland Fire") plans to start play in the [Trail Blazers](/wiki/Portland_Trail_Blazers "Portland Trail Blazers")' home of [Moda Center](/wiki/Moda_Center "Moda Center"), but does not share ownership with the NBA team; the WNBA team instead shares ownership with the [Portland Thorns](/wiki/Portland_Thorns_FC "Portland Thorns FC") of the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League").

#### Do not share market with an NBA team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Do not share market with an NBA team")]

Three teams do not share a market with an NBA counterpart. The [Orlando Miracle](/wiki/Orlando_Miracle "Orlando Miracle") was the sister team of the [Magic](/wiki/Orlando_Magic "Orlando Magic") until the team was sold to the [Mohegan Sun](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun "Mohegan Sun") casino, who relocated the franchise to [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") to become the [Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun").

The [Utah Starzz](/wiki/Utah_Starzz "Utah Starzz") were affiliated with the [Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz") before relocating to San Antonio as the [Silver Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars") in 2003. The Silver Stars (shortened to Stars in 2014) were then paired with the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") from 2003 through the 2017 season, but that relationship ended in October 2017 when the Stars were bought by [MGM Resorts International](/wiki/MGM_Resorts_International "MGM Resorts International") and moved to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") to become the [Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces").[[184]](#cite_note-Stars_move2-185)

The [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") was formerly the sister team of the [SuperSonics](/wiki/Seattle_SuperSonics "Seattle SuperSonics"), but was sold to a Seattle-based group when the SuperSonics [relocated](/wiki/Seattle_SuperSonics_relocation_to_Oklahoma_City "Seattle SuperSonics relocation to Oklahoma City") to become the [Oklahoma City Thunder](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Thunder "Oklahoma City Thunder").

## Season format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Season format")]

### Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Regular season")]

Teams hold training camps in April and May that allow the coaching staff to prepare the players for the regular season and determine the 12-woman roster with which they will begin the regular season. A series of preseason games is also played during this period.[[185]](#cite_note-186)[[186]](#cite_note-187)

The WNBA regular season begins in May. In 2023 and 2024, each team played 40 regular-season games, 20 each home and away. With the [Golden State Valkyries](/wiki/Golden_State_Valkyries "Golden State Valkyries") joining the league in 2025, the regular season expanded to 44 games.[[187]](#cite_note-2025format-188) As in the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), each team hosts and visits every other team at least once every season.

During years in which the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") are held, the WNBA takes a month off in the middle of the season to allow players to practice and compete with their respective national teams. During years in which the [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup") is held, the WNBA either takes a break for the World Cup or ends its season early, depending on the scheduling of the World Cup.

### Commissioner's Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Commissioner's Cup")]

Main article: [WNBA Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "WNBA Commissioner's Cup")

The 2020 season was planned to be the first for the [Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "WNBA Commissioner's Cup"), an in-season tournament. Each team's first home and away games against each of its conference opponents, all of which were to be played in the first half of the season, were designated as Cup games. After each team played its 10 Cup games, the top team in each conference's Cup standings would advance to the Commissioner's Cup Final, a single match held in August. The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") caused the tournament to be scrapped for the time being; the tournament instead launched in 2021 under the originally announced format.[[188]](#cite_note-189)

The format was changed for the 2024 season. Each team now plays only one Commissioner's Cup game against each team in its conference, with either two or three games at home and the remainder away. All games in the first half of June are Cup contests. The Cup final, which remains a single game featuring the top teams in each conference's Cup standings, is now held near the end of June or at the start of July. Under both formats, all Cup games except the final count as regular-season games.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

### All-Star Game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: All-Star Game")]

Main article: [WNBA All-Star Game](/wiki/WNBA_All-Star_Game "WNBA All-Star Game")

In July, the regular season pauses to celebrate the annual All-Star Game. The first edition was held in [1999](/wiki/1999_WNBA_All-Star_Game "1999 WNBA All-Star Game"), the league's third season. The game is part of a weekend-long event, held in a selected WNBA city each year and played on the selected WNBA team's home court. Through the 2017 edition, the All-Star Game featured star players from the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(WNBA) "Western Conference (WNBA)") facing star players from the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(WNBA) "Eastern Conference (WNBA)"). Since 2018, conference affiliations have been ignored in team selections. During the season, voting for All-Star starters takes place among fans, WNBA players, and sports media members. The starters are selected by a weighted vote (fans 50%, players and media 25% each), while reserves are selected by the league's head coaches. The two players with the most fan votes are named team captains, who then fill out their teams in a draft format.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

In 2004, [The Game at Radio City](/wiki/The_Game_at_Radio_City "The Game at Radio City") was held in place of a traditional All-Star Game. The 2006 All-Star Game was the first game to feature custom uniforms that match the decade anniversary logo. From 2008 through 2016, no All-Star Game was held in any Summer Olympic year. In 2010, an exhibition game ([Stars at the Sun](/wiki/Stars_at_the_Sun "Stars at the Sun")) was held. Although the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") were postponed to 2021 due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), no All-Star Game was played in that season. The 2021 season featured the first [All-Star Game](/wiki/2021_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2021 WNBA All-Star Game") in an Olympic year since 2000; this contest featured a WNBA all-star team facing the [US national team](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_basketball_team "United States women's national basketball team"). The [2024 game](/wiki/2024_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2024 WNBA All-Star Game") used the same format as in 2021, and was also an official All-Star Game.

Shortly after the All-Star break is the [trading deadline](/wiki/Trading_deadline "Trading deadline"). After this date, teams are not allowed to exchange players with each other for the remainder of the season, although they may still sign and release players. Major trades are often completed right before the trading deadline.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

### Playoffs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Playoffs")]

Main article: [WNBA playoffs](/wiki/WNBA_playoffs "WNBA playoffs")

The WNBA playoffs usually begin in late September, though in years of the [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup") they begin in August. In the current system, the eight best teams by the regular-season record, without regard to conference alignment, qualify for the playoffs. Since 2022, the playoffs have been held in a standard knockout format, with the quarterfinals consisting of best-of-three series and the semifinals being best-of-five. The Finals were also best-of-five until being expanded to best-of-seven in 2025.[[192]](#cite_note-2022format-193) Since 2021, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") has been the official sponsor of all playoff rounds except for the Finals,[[193]](#cite_note-194) which are sponsored by [YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV").[[187]](#cite_note-2025format-188)

Having a higher seed offers several advantages. The higher seed will generally face a weaker team, and will have home-court advantage in each round. In 2024, all quarterfinal series used a 2–1 home-court pattern, which allowed the higher seed the opportunity to win the series without having to visit the lower seed. This in turn meant that a lower seed that won one of the first two games would host the series decider.[[192]](#cite_note-2022format-193) However, the quarterfinals returned to an odd-even pattern in 2025, with the higher seed hosting the first game and a potential third game.[[187]](#cite_note-2025format-188)

The quarterfinals are bracketed in the normal manner for an 8-team tournament, with 1 vs. 8 and 4 vs. 5 on one side of the bracket and 2 vs. 7 and 3 vs. 6 on the other. The winners of each quarterfinal series advance to the semifinals, with the bracket not being reseeded. The semifinals use a 2–2–1 home-court pattern, meaning that the higher-seeded team will have home court in games 1, 2, and 5 while the other team plays at home in game 3 and 4.

### Finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Finals")]

Main article: [WNBA Finals](/wiki/WNBA_Finals "WNBA Finals")

The final playoff round, a best-of-seven series between the two semifinal winners, is known as the WNBA Finals and is held annually, currently scheduled in October. Each player on the winning team receives a championship ring. Also, the league awards a [WNBA Finals Most Valuable Player Award](/wiki/WNBA_Finals_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "WNBA Finals Most Valuable Player Award"). Since 2025, the Finals have used the same 2–2–1–1–1 pattern currently used by the NBA, meaning that the higher seed hosts games 1 and 2, the lower seed hosts games 3 and 4, and the teams alternate the last three games, all if necessary.[[194]](#cite_note-2025format2-195)

## Players and coaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Players and coaches")]

Main articles: [List of Women's National Basketball Association players](/wiki/List_of_Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association_players "List of Women's National Basketball Association players") and [List of Women's National Basketball Association head coaches](/wiki/List_of_Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association_head_coaches "List of Women's National Basketball Association head coaches")

See also: [List of foreign WNBA players](/wiki/List_of_foreign_WNBA_players "List of foreign WNBA players")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/MN_Lynx%27s_coach_Cheryl_Reeve_in_2024_%281%29.jpg/250px-MN_Lynx%27s_coach_Cheryl_Reeve_in_2024_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MN_Lynx%27s_coach_Cheryl_Reeve_in_2024_(1).jpg)

[Cheryl Reeve](/wiki/Cheryl_Reeve "Cheryl Reeve") Head Coach and President of Basketball Operations of the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx"), known for her successful coaching career and leadership in the WNBA.

In 2011, a decade and a half after the launch of the WNBA, only two players remained from the league's [inaugural season in 1997](/wiki/1997_WNBA_season "1997 WNBA season"): [Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes") and [Tina Thompson](/wiki/Tina_Thompson "Tina Thompson"). [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie") was the longest-tenured player from the 1997 draft class; she spent her entire career (1997–2009) with the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks"). [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird") holds both of the league's most significant longevity records—number of seasons in the league (19) and games played (580).

The members of the [WNBA's All-Decade Team](/wiki/WNBA%27s_All-Decade_Team "WNBA's All-Decade Team") were chosen in 2006 on the occasion of the tenth anniversary of the WNBA from 30 nominees compiled by fans, media, coach, and player voting. The team was to comprise the 10 best and most influential players of the first decade of the WNBA, with consideration also given to sportsmanship, community service, leadership, and contribution to the growth of women's basketball. Players for the [WNBA's Top 15 Team](/wiki/WNBA%27s_Top_15_Team "WNBA's Top 15 Team") were chosen in 2011 on the anniversary of the league's fifteenth season from amongst 30 nominees compiled similarly to that of the All-Decade Team process. This process was repeated for the league's 20th anniversary season in 2016 with the selection of the [WNBA Top 20@20](/wiki/WNBA_Top_20@20 "WNBA Top 20@20"), and for the 25th anniversary season in 2021 with the selection of [The W25](/wiki/The_W25 "The W25").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Micheletimms2016.jpg/250px-Micheletimms2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Micheletimms2016.jpg)

[Michele Timms](/wiki/Michele_Timms "Michele Timms"), known for her skillful playmaking and leadership on the court.

Over [30 players](/wiki/WNBA_Career_Scoring "WNBA Career Scoring") have scored at least 3,000 points in their WNBA careers. Only 15 WNBA players have reached the 6,000 point milestone: [Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi"), [Tina Charles](/wiki/Tina_Charles_(basketball) "Tina Charles (basketball)"), [DeWanna Bonner](/wiki/DeWanna_Bonner "DeWanna Bonner"), [Tina Thompson](/wiki/Tina_Thompson "Tina Thompson"), [Tamika Catchings](/wiki/Tamika_Catchings "Tamika Catchings"), [Nneka Ogwumike](/wiki/Nneka_Ogwumike "Nneka Ogwumike"), [Candice Dupree](/wiki/Candice_Dupree "Candice Dupree"), [Cappie Pondexter](/wiki/Cappie_Pondexter "Cappie Pondexter"), [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird"), [Candance Parker](/wiki/Candace_Parker "Candace Parker"), [Katie Smith](/wiki/Katie_Smith "Katie Smith"), [Sylvia Fowles](/wiki/Sylvia_Fowles "Sylvia Fowles"), [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie"), [Seimone Augustus](/wiki/Seimone_Augustus "Seimone Augustus") and [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson").[[195]](#cite_note-196) The scoring average leader is A'ja Wilson of the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces"), with an average of 21.30 points per game.[[196]](#cite_note-197)

In 2007, [Paul Westhead](/wiki/Paul_Westhead "Paul Westhead") of the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") became the first person to earn both [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and WNBA championship rings as a coach.

In 2008, 50-year-old [Nancy Lieberman](/wiki/Nancy_Lieberman "Nancy Lieberman") became the oldest player to play in a WNBA game. She signed a seven-day contract with the [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") and played one game, tallying [two assists and two turnovers](https://web.archive.org/web/20080912150859/http://www.wnba.com/games/20080724/DETHOU/boxscore.html) in nine minutes of action. By playing in the one game Lieberman broke a record that she had set in 1997 when she was the league's oldest player at 39. The oldest player to have participated in a full season is [Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi"), who turned 42 early in the [2024 season](/wiki/2024_WNBA_season "2024 WNBA season").

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Awards")]

Around the beginning of September (or late August in Olympic and [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup") years), the regular season ends. It is during this time that voting begins for individual awards. The [Sixth Player of the Year Award](/wiki/WNBA_Sixth_Player_of_the_Year_Award "WNBA Sixth Player of the Year Award") (known before 2021 as the "Sixth Woman" award) is given to the best player coming off the bench (must have more games coming off the bench than actual games started).[[199]](#cite_note-WNBA6th-200) The [Rookie of the Year Award](/wiki/WNBA_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "WNBA Rookie of the Year Award") is awarded to the most outstanding first-year player. The [Most Improved Player Award](/wiki/WNBA_Most_Improved_Player_Award "WNBA Most Improved Player Award") is awarded to the player who is deemed to have shown the most improvement from the previous season. The [Defensive Player of the Year Award](/wiki/WNBA_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award "WNBA Defensive Player of the Year Award") is awarded to the league's best defender. The [Kim Perrot Sportsmanship Award](/wiki/Kim_Perrot_Sportsmanship_Award "Kim Perrot Sportsmanship Award") is awarded to the player who shows outstanding sportsmanship on and off the court. The [Coach of the Year Award](/wiki/WNBA_Coach_of_the_Year_Award "WNBA Coach of the Year Award") is awarded to the coach that has made the most positive difference to a team. The [Most Valuable Player Award](/wiki/WNBA_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "WNBA Most Valuable Player Award") is given to the player deemed the most valuable for her team that season. The [Basketball Executive of the Year Award](/wiki/WNBA_Basketball_Executive_of_the_Year_Award "WNBA Basketball Executive of the Year Award") is presented to the team executive most instrumental in his or her team's success in that season. The newest WNBA award, first presented in 2019, is the season-long version of the WNBA Community Assist Award, presented to a player for especially meritorious community service.[[a]](#cite_note-202)

Also named are the [All-WNBA Teams](/wiki/All-WNBA_Team "All-WNBA Team"), the [All-Defensive Teams](/wiki/WNBA_All-Defensive_Team "WNBA All-Defensive Team"), and the [All-Rookie Team](/wiki/WNBA_All-Rookie_Team "WNBA All-Rookie Team"); each consists of five players. There are two All-WNBA teams; starting with the 2022 season, each consists of five top players selected without regard to position, with first-team status being the most desirable.[[201]](#cite_note-203) There are two All-Defensive teams; since the 2023 season, each consists of the top defenders regardless of position. Finally, there is one All-Rookie team, consisting of the top five first-year players regardless of position. (In all cases, a tie in voting may lead to a team containing six players instead of five.)

## Rules and regulations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Rules and regulations")]

Rules are governed by standard [basketball rules](/wiki/Basketball#Rules_and_regulations "Basketball") as defined by the NBA, with a few notable exceptions:

* The three-point line is 22 ft 1.75 in (6.75 m) from the center of the basket, with a distance of 22 ft (6.71 m) at the corners. The main arc is essentially identical to that used by [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") (effective October 1, 2012, for domestic competitions) and the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (effective in 2019–20 in Division I and 2020–21 in Divisions II and III for men, and in 2021–22 for all women's divisions). The WNBA corner distance, as measured from the center of the basket, is identical to that of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"); the FIBA and NCAA distance at the corners is 4 in (10.16 cm) shorter.
* The regulation WNBA ball is a minimum 28.5 inches (72 cm) in circumference and weighs 20.0 ounces (570 g), 1 inch (2.5 cm) smaller and 2 ounces (57 g) lighter than the NBA ball. Since 2004, this size has been used for all senior-level women's competitions throughout the world in full-court basketball. Competitions in the half-court [3x3](/wiki/3x3_basketball "3x3 basketball") variant used the women's ball until 2015, when a dedicated ball with the circumference of the women's ball but the weight of the men's ball was introduced. [Wilson](/wiki/Wilson_Sporting_Goods "Wilson Sporting Goods") became the WNBA ball supplier in 2021. Prior to that year, [Spalding](/wiki/Spalding_(company) "Spalding (company)") had been the ball supplier since 1997.
* Quarters are 10 minutes in duration instead of 12.

Games are divided into four 10-minute quarters as opposed to the league's original two 20-minute halves of play, similar to FIBA and NCAA women's college rules.

A trend with new WNBA rules has been to match them with a similar NBA rule. Since the [2006 WNBA season](/wiki/2006_WNBA_season "2006 WNBA season"):[[236]](#cite_note-WNBA_Announces_Rule_Changes_for_2006-244)

* The winner of the opening jump ball shall begin the 4th quarter with the ball out of bounds. The loser shall begin with the ball out of bounds in the second and third quarters. Previously under the two-half format, both periods started with jump balls, presumably to eliminate the possibility of a team intentionally losing the opening tip to gain the opening possession of the second half. This is not a problem under the four-quarters because the winner of the opening tip gets the opening possession of the final period.
* The [shot clock](/wiki/Shot_clock "Shot clock") was decreased from 30 to 24 seconds, matching the FIBA shot clock. Starting in 2020, the last five seconds of the shot clock counted down in tenths of a second.

The [2007 WNBA season](/wiki/2007_WNBA_season "2007 WNBA season") brought changes that included:[[237]](#cite_note-WNBA_Announces_Rule_Changes_for_2007_Season-245)

* The amount of time that a team must move the ball across the half-court line went from 10 to 8 seconds.
* A referee can grant time-outs to either a player or the coach.
* Two free throws and possession of the ball for a clear-path-to-the-basket foul. Previously only one free throw was awarded as well as possession.

In [2012](/wiki/2012_WNBA_season "2012 WNBA season"), the WNBA added the block/charge arc under the basket. As of [2013](/wiki/2013_WNBA_season "2013 WNBA season") the defensive three-second rule and anti-flopping guidelines were introduced. The three-point line was also extended; in 2017, that line extended into the corners to match the NBA's.

Since [2017](/wiki/2017_WNBA_season "2017 WNBA season"), [Tissot](/wiki/Tissot "Tissot") is the official timekeeper for the league, as it uses a unified game clock/shot clock system.

## Business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Business")]

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Finance")]

In 2007, teams were estimated to be losing $1.5 million to $2 million a year.[[238]](#cite_note-246) In total, the league projected losses through the 2010 season to be around $400 million.[[239]](#cite_note-247) In December 2010, [Donna Orender](/wiki/Donna_Orender "Donna Orender") said that the league had its first-ever "cash flow positive" team during the 2010 season.[[240]](#cite_note-248) In 2011, three teams were profitable, and in 2013, six of the league's 12 teams reported a profit.[[241]](#cite_note-249) However, in 2018, NBA Commissioner [Adam Silver](/wiki/Adam_Silver "Adam Silver") revealed that the WNBA had lost an average of more than $10 million annually, which would amount to at least $280 million in losses since the WNBA's inception in 1997.[[242]](#cite_note-Dixon-250)

By the 2024 season, the WNBA has seen their revenue grow rapidly through a variety of avenues, such as through raising capital,[[243]](#cite_note-251) re-negotiating their media rights deal, expansion fees,[[244]](#cite_note-:7-252) increased ticket sales,[[245]](#cite_note-:6-253) and establishing new strategic partnerships and sponsorships with organizations.[[246]](#cite_note-:8-254) Still, 40% of the revenue generated by the WNBA goes to the teams and the players, with the remainder going to the NBA and outside investors. Overall, the league is expected to have lost $50 million for the 2024 season.[[244]](#cite_note-:7-252)

### Activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Activism")]

Main article: [WNBA Social Justice Council](/wiki/WNBA_Social_Justice_Council "WNBA Social Justice Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/New_York_Liberty_WNBA_Team_staff_wearing_Black_Lives_Matter_T-Shirts_during_a_game_on_June_19%2C2022.png/250px-New_York_Liberty_WNBA_Team_staff_wearing_Black_Lives_Matter_T-Shirts_during_a_game_on_June_19%2C2022.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_Liberty_WNBA_Team_staff_wearing_Black_Lives_Matter_T-Shirts_during_a_game_on_June_19,2022.png)

New York Liberty WNBA Team staff wearing Black Lives Matter T-Shirts during a game on June 19, 2022

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* in 2020 called the WNBA "the most socially progressive pro league".[[247]](#cite_note-255)

As the popularity of the league has grown, players have gained more voice and power to perform as activists in many fields. One of the activist players' main focuses is the inequality between men's and women's sports. Many players such as [Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner"), [Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart"), and [Maya Moore](/wiki/Maya_Moore "Maya Moore") have spoken about equality between gender, sexual orientation, and race.[[248]](#cite_note-256) The players have also supported progressive social and political movements such as [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") and others.[[249]](#cite_note-257) The [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") were early advocates. *The New York Times* called [Seimone Augustus](/wiki/Seimone_Augustus "Seimone Augustus") "one of sports' most forward-thinking and undersung activists" for her early work advocating for [marriage equality](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") and [LGBTQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ") rights.[[250]](#cite_note-258) Before the Lynx July 9, 2016, home game, the team held a press conference where the four co-captains wore black t-shirts with the message ""Change starts with us - Justice and Accountability" on the front and the names of [Philando Castile](/wiki/Killing_of_Philando_Castile "Killing of Philando Castile") and [Alton Sterling](/wiki/Killing_of_Alton_Sterling "Killing of Alton Sterling") on the back. Castile had been killed by police in a traffic stop three days before the game.[[251]](#cite_note-259) Shortly after the [George Floyd protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests") began, the league and union decided in 2020 to put Black Lives Matter and [Say Her Name](/wiki/Say_Her_Name "Say Her Name") slogans on warmup gear and opening weekend uniforms.[[252]](#cite_note-npr_dream-260) When team owner Senator [Kelly Loeffler](/wiki/Kelly_Loeffler "Kelly Loeffler") criticized the league's support for Black Lives Matter, her team wore black T-shirts with the slogan "VOTE WARNOCK", endorsing her election opponent [Raphael Warnock](/wiki/Raphael_Warnock "Raphael Warnock"), an African-American pastor who defeated Loeffler.[[252]](#cite_note-npr_dream-260)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/WNBA_Activism_VOTE.jpg/250px-WNBA_Activism_VOTE.jpg)](/wiki/File:WNBA_Activism_VOTE.jpg)

Players from the Las Vegas Aces in September 2024, wearing T-shirts that read VOTE.

In 2021, during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports"), the WNBA led American professional sports teams in promoting the [COVID-19 vaccine](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine").[[253]](#cite_note-:1-261) Teams hosted vaccine clinics in their home arenas.[[253]](#cite_note-:1-261) In April the league and union's Social Justice Council made a [PSA](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement"), *Our Health is Worth a Shot*, that aired during the WNBA draft.[[253]](#cite_note-:1-261)[[254]](#cite_note-262) In June 2021, the WNBA announced that 99% of its players had been fully vaccinated.[[255]](#cite_note-263)

In June 2024, the documentary film *[Power of the Dream](/wiki/Power_of_the_Dream "Power of the Dream")* was released.[[256]](#cite_note-:4-264) The film focuses on the league's work to support Black Lives Matter and the organizing to support the election of Warnock. [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird") and [Nneka Ogwumike](/wiki/Nneka_Ogwumike "Nneka Ogwumike") are co-producers of the film along with [Tracee Ellis Ross](/wiki/Tracee_Ellis_Ross "Tracee Ellis Ross") and the director [Dawn Porter](/wiki/Dawn_Porter_(filmmaker) "Dawn Porter (filmmaker)").[[256]](#cite_note-:4-264)

### Sponsorships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Sponsorships")]

On June 1, 2009, the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") was the first team in WNBA history to announce a marquee sponsorship.[[257]](#cite_note-265) The team secured a partnership with [LifeLock](/wiki/LifeLock "LifeLock") to brand their jerseys and warm-ups.[[258]](#cite_note-266) It was the first branded jersey in WNBA history. Following the expiration of the LifeLock deal, the Mercury secured a new uniform sponsorship deal with [Casino Arizona](/wiki/Casino_Arizona "Casino Arizona") and [Talking Stick Resort](/wiki/Talking_Stick_Resort "Talking Stick Resort") on February 3, 2014.[[259]](#cite_note-267)

Other teams eventually followed in the Mercury's footsteps; some teams feature sponsors prominently on the front of their jerseys, while others have sponsors on the upper left-hand shoulder.[[260]](#cite_note-268)

On August 22, 2011, the WNBA announced a league-wide marquee sponsorship with [Boost Mobile](/wiki/Boost_Mobile "Boost Mobile").[[261]](#cite_note-269) The deal would allow the Boost Mobile logo to be placed on eleven of the 12 teams' jerseys (excluding San Antonio) in addition to branding on the courts and in arenas. A source said the deal is a "multiyear, eight-figure deal".[[262]](#cite_note-270)

Before the start of the 2011 season, every team announced a new look for their uniforms. The supplier of the uniforms for the league, [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), upgraded all teams to new high-tech designs, much like they did for the NBA before the start of their season.

On April 8, 2019, the WNBA announced a multiyear marquee partnership with [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T"), making them the first non-apparel partner to have its logo featured on the front of all 12 team jerseys. The jerseys officially debuted during the [2019 WNBA draft](/wiki/2019_WNBA_draft "2019 WNBA draft").[[263]](#cite_note-271)

In 2020, the league launched WNBA Changemakers, a collective of businesses committed to the advancement of women in sports. The business partners that make up the collective provide direct financial investment to the WNBA as well as marketing amplification through collaborations.[[246]](#cite_note-:8-254)

### Salaries, rosters, and collective bargaining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Salaries, rosters, and collective bargaining")]

Before the 2009 season, the maximum team roster size was changed from 13 players (11 active and 2 inactive) to 11 players (all active). Any team that falls below nine players able to play due to injury or any other factor outside of the control of the team will, upon request, be granted a roster hardship exception allowing the team to sign an additional player or players so that the team will have nine players able to play in an upcoming game or games. As soon as the injured (or otherwise sidelined) player(s) can play, the roster hardship player(s)—not any other player on the roster—must be waived. In March 2014, the WNBA and players signed a new, eight-year collective bargaining agreement, increasing the number of players on a roster to 12.[[264]](#cite_note-272)

The [WNBA draft](/wiki/WNBA_draft "WNBA draft") is held annually every spring. The minimum age is 22 years for American players and 20 years for international players, measured as of December 31 of the calendar year of the draft. For draft purposes, "American" includes those born in the U.S., as well as those who have enrolled in a U.S. college or university, regardless of their citizenship. The draft is three rounds long, with each of the 12 teams in the league (trades aside) getting three picks each. The draft order for the eight teams that made the playoffs the previous year are based on team records, and the team with the highest previous record will pick last. For the remaining top four picks, a selection process similar to the [NBA draft lottery](/wiki/NBA_draft_lottery "NBA draft lottery") is conducted for the four teams that did not qualify for the playoffs.

Previously, in 2008, a new six-year collective bargaining agreement was agreed upon between the players and the league. The [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") for an entire team in 2010 was $827,000 (although it was later lowered to $775,000). By 2013 (the sixth year under this agreement), the cap for an entire team was $900,000. In 2010, the *minimum* salary for a player with three-plus years of experience was $51,000 while the *maximum* salary for a six-plus year player was $101,500 (the first time in league history that players can receive over $100,000). The minimum salary for rookies was $35,190.[[265]](#cite_note-273)[[266]](#cite_note-274) Many WNBA players supplement their salaries by playing in [European](/wiki/European_Women%27s_Basketball_League "European Women's Basketball League"), [Australian](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_League "Women's National Basketball League"), or more recently [Chinese](/wiki/Women%27s_Chinese_Basketball_Association "Women's Chinese Basketball Association") women's basketball leagues during the WNBA offseason. The WNBA [pays its female players less](/wiki/Gender_pay_gap "Gender pay gap") than their NBA counterparts, although this is attributed to the much greater revenues of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"); however the WNBA has been criticized for "paying its players a lower share of revenue than the NBA".[[267]](#cite_note-275)

The decision of superstar [Diana Taurasi](/wiki/Diana_Taurasi "Diana Taurasi") to sit out the 2015 WNBA season was seen by some in the media as a harbinger of salary-related troubles in the future. The Russian club for which she was playing at the time, [UMMC Ekaterinburg](/wiki/UMMC_Ekaterinburg "UMMC Ekaterinburg"), offered her a bonus well over the league's maximum player salary to sit out that season. Taurasi accepted, largely because she had not had an offseason since playing college basketball more than a decade earlier. Such offers have often been made to star American players, including Taurasi herself, but none were accepted until Taurasi did so in 2015.[[268]](#cite_note-276)

A more recent incident that led to widespread media comment on the WNBA's salary structure was the [torn](/wiki/Achilles_tendon_rupture "Achilles tendon rupture") [Achilles](/wiki/Achilles_tendon "Achilles tendon") suffered by reigning WNBA MVP [Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart") while playing for another Russian side, [Dynamo Kursk](/wiki/Dynamo_Kursk "Dynamo Kursk"), in the [2019 EuroLeague Women final](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_EuroLeague_Women "2018–19 EuroLeague Women"). The injury came at a time when the WNBA and its players' union were preparing to negotiate a new collective bargaining agreement,[[269]](#cite_note-277) following the union's announcement in November 2018 that it would opt out of the current CBA after the 2019 season. With overseas leagues offering much higher salaries to many players than the WNBA currently provides, roughly 70% of the league's players go overseas in any given season. While these players do not necessarily play as many games as NBA players do in their seasons, even participants in the [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals") get several months of rest in the offseason, something not available for WNBA players who also play overseas.[[270]](#cite_note-278)[[271]](#cite_note-279)[[272]](#cite_note-Voepel_Stewie-280) In a story on the ramifications of Stewart's injury, [Michael Voepel](/wiki/Michael_Voepel "Michael Voepel") of [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") had this to say about the lead-in to the injury:[[272]](#cite_note-Voepel_Stewie-280)

> For Stewart, her 2018 went like this: playing in [China](/wiki/Women%27s_Chinese_Basketball_Association "Women's Chinese Basketball Association"), brief time off, [WNBA season](/wiki/2018_WNBA_season "2018 WNBA season"), [World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "2018 FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup") in the Canary Islands, brief time off, playing in Russia. She hasn't had significant recovery time since before her [senior season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_UConn_Huskies_women%27s_basketball_team "2015–16 UConn Huskies women's basketball team") at [UConn](/wiki/UConn_Huskies_women%27s_basketball "UConn Huskies women's basketball"). Now, she'll have time away from playing but while going through rehab and physical therapy.

The current CBA, which took effect in 2020, significantly increased minimum and maximum salaries. The minimum league salary in 2020 was $57,000 for players with less than three years of experience, and $68,000 otherwise. For most players, the 2020 maximum salary was $185,000; players who met specified criteria for league service had a maximum of $215,000.[[273]](#cite_note-281)

WNBA players are awarded bonuses for certain achievements. Some of the bonuses given by the league (amount is per player), from 2020 to 2027 (the duration of the current CBA):
WNBA champion: $11,356; Runner-up: $5,678; Most Valuable Player: $15,450; All-WNBA First Team member: $10,300; and All-Star Game participant: $2,575.[[274]](#cite_note-282) These were only modest increases from amounts provided before 2020.[[275]](#cite_note-283)

In recent years, the lack of roster space for rookies, thus hampering their professional development, has become a major issue. While the 2020 CBA led to maximum player salaries nearly doubling from 2019 to 2022, the team cap only increased by slightly less than 40% in that period. Because the cap is a hard cap, many WNBA teams now carry only 11 players on their rosters instead of the maximum 12, leading Stewart to state "We're at a tipping point. . . . without some easy tweaks, we are no longer a league that has 12 teams and 144 players — it's more like 133." The WNBA also has no developmental league similar to the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League"); [Chiney Ogwumike](/wiki/Chiney_Ogwumike "Chiney Ogwumike"), a vice president of the players' union, publicly called for such a league. While roster limits have always been a significant issue in the league—between the first WNBA draft in 1997 and 2021, more than 40% of drafted players never made a roster—this became especially apparent at the dawn of the 2020s. In one notable example, the [2019](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_season "2018–19 NCAA Division I women's basketball season") [Naismith Trophy](/wiki/Naismith_College_Player_of_the_Year "Naismith College Player of the Year") winner [Megan Gustafson](/wiki/Megan_Gustafson "Megan Gustafson") did not make an opening-day roster in 2019, and had only played in parts of the 2019–2021 seasons before being cut in the 2022 preseason. This issue gained major publicity during the week before the start of the 2022 season. The [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx"), which began that week with barely over $12,000 of cap room, cut six players, including the 2020 Rookie of the Year [Crystal Dangerfield](/wiki/Crystal_Dangerfield "Crystal Dangerfield"), their 2021 first-round pick [Rennia Davis](/wiki/Rennia_Davis "Rennia Davis"), and both of their 2022 draft picks. The [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm"), whose first 2022 draft pick was in the middle of the second round, waived that pick ([Elissa Cunane](/wiki/Elissa_Cunane "Elissa Cunane")), and the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces") waived both of their picks, one of them a first-rounder.[[276]](#cite_note-284)[[277]](#cite_note-285)

Another clause in the 2020 CBA, known as the "prioritization" clause, has been viewed as a potential problem for the league. Because of overseas league commitments, a significant number of WNBA players have reported late to training camp each season. Several overseas leagues and continental club competitions overlap with WNBA training camps, and even with the start of the WNBA season. For example, 55 WNBA players missed the start of training camp in 2021, meaning that most teams were unable to start practice with their full rosters.[[278]](#cite_note-Hruby-286) Starting in 2023, teams are required to fine players with more than two years of WNBA experience who miss the start of training camp. (Exemptions are provided for national team commitments, graduations, and other significant life events.) Starting in 2024, the league can penalize a veteran player who does not report to camp with a season-long suspension without pay.[[279]](#cite_note-Negley20220211-287) In a 2021 episode of a podcast hosted by [Napheesa Collier](/wiki/Napheesa_Collier "Napheesa Collier") and [A'ja Wilson](/wiki/A%27ja_Wilson "A'ja Wilson"), Collier raised the prospect of players choosing to abandon the WNBA for higher overseas salaries, telling Wilson "If I'm not making that much in the league, if it's not enough for me to survive on during the year, I'm going overseas and having the summer off."[[278]](#cite_note-Hruby-286) When Stewart re-signed with the Seattle Storm as a free agent before the 2022 season, she cited the prioritization clause as the reason she only signed a one-year contract.[[279]](#cite_note-Negley20220211-287)

#### Player revenue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Player revenue")]

There are many factors that play into the smaller wages WNBA players receive during their season, but two major contributors are revenue and viewership numbers and the allocation of funds by both the NBA and the WNBA. It is estimated that wages for NBA players take up 50% of the league's annual revenue, while WNBA players only receive 20% of their league's income.[[280]](#cite_note-:13-288) Kelsey Plum said in an interview with The Residency Podcast, "We're not asking to get paid what the men get paid. We're asking to get paid the same percentage of revenue shared."[[281]](#cite_note-289)[[282]](#cite_note-290)

#### Player marketing agreements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Player marketing agreements")]

Player marketing agreements (PMAs), introduced in the 2021–22 offseason, are league contracts that pay select WNBA players for year‑round marketing and promotional work on behalf of the league.[[283]](#cite_note-:22-291)[[284]](#cite_note-292) The league was expected to spend about $1 million annually on PMAs, with funds divided among participating players and individual contracts capped at $250,000. PMAs provide additional offseason income and are also intended to encourage players to remain stateside. Players have cited varied reasons for signing PMAs, including the need to rest or rehabilitate injuries and childcare responsibilities.[[283]](#cite_note-:22-291) However, most top WNBA players have opted not to sign PMAs, citing concerns that the agreements limit other name, image, and likeness opportunities.[[285]](#cite_note-293)

#### NBA support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: NBA support")]

The WNBA is half-owned by the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and receives between $10–$15 million annually to [subsidize](/wiki/Subsidy "Subsidy") the league from the NBA's yearly revenue, which in 2022, reached up to $10 billion.[[286]](#cite_note-294) Six WNBA teams are considered sister teams to the NBA teams based in the same areas and are directly connected to those NBA teams and their markets. The NBA's allotment of funding to all WNBA teams, not just sister teams, has been discussed in many academic and sports analysis circles, with some authors arguing that the NBA should be doing more to support the WNBA if they want the league to succeed.[[287]](#cite_note-:02-295) [Sponsorship](/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)") and television deals continue to be an area where the WNBA creates less revenue than their NBA counterparts, with the WNBA bringing in $25 million from ESPN and the NBA bringing in $930 million from [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)"). Some argue that the NBA could be working harder to bridge that gap between organizations and either share more revenue from deals or help the WNBA create their own more profitable agreements.[[287]](#cite_note-:02-295)

In July 2024, the WNBA negotiated a new media rights deal as part of a total package with the NBA valued at $77 billion. Prior to re-negotiating their deal, the WNBA media rights were only valued at $60 million a year. Under the new deal, the WNBA can expect to earn $200 million a year for the next eleven years, more than a 300% increase over previous years,[[288]](#cite_note-sherwood.news-296) but because the media deal lumps the NBA together with the WNBA, the true valuation of the WNBA media rights remains unknown.[[244]](#cite_note-:7-252)

### Merchandise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Merchandise")]

#### 2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: 2021")]

The following shows the top merchandise sales during the [2021 season](/wiki/2021_WNBA_season "2021 WNBA season"), based on sales through the WNBA's official online store.[[289]](#cite_note-2021_jerseys-297)

However, a story by [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Sports "NBC Sports") journalist Alex Azzi argued that the WNBA's merchandise rankings were misleading for multiple reasons.[[290]](#cite_note-298)

* At any given time, the WNBA has at most 144 players, but at the time of the story, "ready to ship" replica team jerseys were available for fewer than 20 of them. Many of the league's top players in the 2021 season were not among those with "ready to ship" jerseys. The only such jersey available for [Jonquel Jones](/wiki/Jonquel_Jones "Jonquel Jones"), who would be named [league MVP](/wiki/WNBA_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "WNBA Most Valuable Player Award") shortly after the story ran, was a replica of the jersey she wore in [that season's All-Star Game](/wiki/2021_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2021 WNBA All-Star Game"), and not her [Connecticut Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun") jersey. The league's leading scorer that season, [Tina Charles](/wiki/Tina_Charles_(basketball) "Tina Charles (basketball)"), had no ready-made jersey available for sale. Also, not all of the ready-made jerseys were available in youth sizes. All other player replica jerseys must be custom-ordered, which take longer to ship, cost more, and are only available in adult sizes. The only [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") player with a ready-made jersey, [Maya Moore](/wiki/Maya_Moore "Maya Moore"), had not played in the league since 2018 (and would formally announce her retirement in 2023).
* Some teams, among them the Lynx, offer a larger variety of ready-made replica jerseys in their online team stores. Also, [Dick's Sporting Goods](/wiki/Dick%27s_Sporting_Goods "Dick's Sporting Goods"), which had entered into a multi-year marketing agreement with the league shortly before the story ran, has a wider availability of such jerseys than the WNBA online store. However, the league's official merchandise rankings do not include sales through any outlets other than its online store.
* While over 80% of WNBA players are black, the top three on this list, as well as four of the top five, are white. A study by two researchers at the [University of Massachusetts](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst") published earlier in 2021 concluded that after controlling for points and rebounds, white players individually averaged twice as much media mentions as black players during the 2020 season.[[291]](#cite_note-299) This discrepancy was specifically called out by white [UConn](/wiki/UConn_Huskies_women%27s_basketball "UConn Huskies women's basketball") superstar [Paige Bueckers](/wiki/Paige_Bueckers "Paige Bueckers") during her acceptance speech at the [2021 ESPY Awards](/wiki/2021_ESPY_Awards "2021 ESPY Awards"), and Azzi argued that it contributed to the perceived racial bias in the WNBA merchandise rankings.

#### 2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: 2024")]

The arrival of [Caitlin Clark](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark "Caitlin Clark") in 2024 [led to unprecedented public interest in the WNBA](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark_effect "Caitlin Clark effect"), and subsequently the league's merchandise was being sold in record numbers, both player-specific and league-wide, such as the orange WNBA hoodie. Clark had the most jerseys sold during the year's first half, with the rankings also featuring two other rookies in [Angel Reese](/wiki/Angel_Reese "Angel Reese") and [Kate Martin](/wiki/Kate_Martin "Kate Martin").[[292]](#cite_note-300)

The title of the league's chief executive was "President" before [Cathy Engelbert](/wiki/Cathy_Engelbert "Cathy Engelbert") became the first "Commissioner".

* [Donna Orender](/wiki/Donna_Orender "Donna Orender"), 2005–2010

## Attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Attendance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/WNBA_attendance.webp/330px-WNBA_attendance.webp.png)](/wiki/File:WNBA_attendance.webp)

WNBA attendance 1997-2023

In the inaugural [1997 WNBA season](/wiki/1997_WNBA_season "1997 WNBA season"), average attendance for the regular season was 9,661 attendants with 112 games played. Average attendance increased by 12% the following year in 1998 over 150 games. By 2000, the regular WNBA season consisted of 256 games and attendance dropped with an average of 9,142 attendants per game for season 2000-2003. Beginning in 2003, attendance dropped at a rate of approximately 5% year-over-year until 2006 when average attendance was 7,479. From 2007 to 2014, attendance numbers remained relatively steady with a low of 7,457 in 2012 and a high of 8,039 in 2009.[[296]](#cite_note-:5-304)

In 2015, the WNBA's attendance per game decreased by 3.4% to 7,318. This was a record low for the WNBA since it was established in 1997. The relocation of the [San Antonio Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars") to [Freeman Coliseum](/wiki/Freeman_Coliseum "Freeman Coliseum") and the [Tulsa Shock](/wiki/Tulsa_Shock "Tulsa Shock") to Arlington likely contributed to particularly large drops in attendance in 2015 (−37.4% and −7.2% respectively). With record low attendance, the WNBA sought to improve their branding effort for the following 20th anniversary season and the WNBA President, [Laurel J. Richie](/wiki/Laurel_J._Richie "Laurel J. Richie"), discussed creating an expansion committee to evaluate if and how the WNBA should go about expanding their reach.[[297]](#cite_note-305)

Attendance in 2016 and 2017 recovered slightly with average attendance at 7,655 and 7,716 respectively, but the 2018 and 2019 seasons each set the lowest average attendance in WNBA history (6,769 and 6,535 respectively). However, about half of the decline in attendance from 2017 to 2018 was due to the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") moving from 19,812-seat [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") to the 5,000-seat [Westchester County Center](/wiki/Westchester_County_Center "Westchester County Center"). While the Liberty had averaged over 9,000 fans in 2017, [James Dolan](/wiki/James_L._Dolan "James L. Dolan"), then the team's owner, noted that roughly half of the team's attendance in that season came from complimentary tickets.[[298]](#cite_note-306) Similarly in 2019, the [Washington Mystics](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics") moved from the 20,356-seat [Capital One Arena](/wiki/Capital_One_Arena "Capital One Arena") to the 4,111-seat [Entertainment and Sports Arena](/wiki/Entertainment_and_Sports_Arena "Entertainment and Sports Arena"). The Las Vegas Aces and New York Liberty each saw double-digit percentage losses in 2019, but half of the league's teams saw attendance increases in that season, and the number of sellouts was the same in both seasons (41).[[299]](#cite_note-SBD_2019-307)

With plans to expand the number of games played by each team from 34 to 36 regular season games, and with the introduction of the mid-season [Commissioner's Cup](/wiki/WNBA_Commissioner%27s_Cup "WNBA Commissioner's Cup") tournament to be broadcast on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and [ABC](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)"), the league had high hopes for the [2020 WNBA season](/wiki/2020_WNBA_season "2020 WNBA season"), but that was derailed when the season was postponed indefinitely due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") on April 3. Instead, the league held an abbreviated 22-game regular season at [IMG Academy](/wiki/IMG_Academy "IMG Academy") in [Bradenton, Florida](/wiki/Bradenton,_Florida "Bradenton, Florida"), with no fans in attendance. The [2021 WNBA season](/wiki/2021_WNBA_season "2021 WNBA season") was also shortened to a 32-game regular season and attendance numbers remained low with an average of 2,620 people in attendance per game, likely due to persistent fears around the [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") virus and new protocols put in place for large indoor venues. By 2022, average attendance had rebounded to 5,646 and in 2023 attendance had recovered to pre-pandemic numbers with 6,615 average attendance per game.[[296]](#cite_note-:5-304)

2024 turned out to be a breakout season for WNBA attendance, with an average of 9,807 fans per game and an increase of 48% over the 2023 season. Three games drew more than 20,000 fans during the 2024 season, including a record-breaking attendance of 20,711 fans when the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") visited the [Washington Mystics](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics") on September 19.[[245]](#cite_note-:6-253) A surge in interest in women's college basketball preceded the [2024 WNBA season](/wiki/2024_WNBA_season "2024 WNBA season"), with viewership of the 2024 women's [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I women's basketball tournament") [championship game](/wiki/2024_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_championship_game "2024 NCAA Division I women's basketball championship game") up over 90% from [the previous season](/wiki/2023_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_championship_game "2023 NCAA Division I women's basketball championship game") and marking the first time in [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") history where viewership for the women's championship game exceeded the [men's](/wiki/2024_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_championship_game "2024 NCAA Division I men's basketball championship game").[[300]](#cite_note-308) Sports analysts cite intense interest in the incoming 2024 rookie class, led by number one draft pick [Caitlin Clark](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark "Caitlin Clark"), as the reason for the dramatic increases in attendance, dubbing her ability to drive up ticket sales "[Clarkonomics](/wiki/Caitlin_Clark_effect "Caitlin Clark effect")".[[301]](#cite_note-309)

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

Main articles: [WNBA on ESPN](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN"), [WNBA on ABC](/wiki/WNBA_on_ABC "WNBA on ABC"), [WNBA on Lifetime](/wiki/WNBA_on_Lifetime "WNBA on Lifetime"), [WNBA on Oxygen](/wiki/WNBA_on_Oxygen "WNBA on Oxygen"), [WNBA on NBC](/wiki/WNBA_on_NBC "WNBA on NBC"), [NBA on Prime Video](/wiki/NBA_on_Prime_Video "NBA on Prime Video"), and [WNBA on CBS](/wiki/WNBA_on_CBS "WNBA on CBS")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Rebecca_Lobo_taken_by_Danny_Karwoski.jpg/250px-Rebecca_Lobo_taken_by_Danny_Karwoski.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rebecca_Lobo_taken_by_Danny_Karwoski.jpg)

[Rebecca Lobo](/wiki/Rebecca_Lobo "Rebecca Lobo"), a former WNBA player, has transitioned into a successful broadcasting career, providing analysis and commentary on WNBA games.

Currently, WNBA games are televised throughout the U.S. by [ABC](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN"), [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), [ESPN2](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN"), and [Ion Television](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television").

In the early years, two women-oriented networks, [Lifetime](/wiki/Lifetime_(TV_network) "Lifetime (TV network)") and [Oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen_(TV_channel) "Oxygen (TV channel)"), also broadcast games, including the first game of the WNBA. [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") showed games from 1997 to 2002 as part of their *[NBA on NBC](/wiki/NBA_on_NBC "NBA on NBC")* coverage before the league transferred the rights to ABC/ESPN.

In June 2007, the WNBA signed a contract extension with ESPN, the first agreement to pay television rights fees to a women's professional league. The new television deal runs from 2009 to 2016. A minimum of 18 games will be broadcast on ABC, ESPN, and [ESPN2](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN") each season; the rights to broadcast the first regular-season game and the All-Star game are held by ABC. Additionally, a minimum of 11 postseason games will be broadcast on any of the three networks.[[309]](#cite_note-332) Along with this deal, came the first-ever rights fees to be paid to a women's professional sports league. Over the eight years of the contract, "millions and millions of dollars" will be "dispersed to the league's teams".[[310]](#cite_note-one-333)

In 2013, the WNBA and ESPN signed a six-year extension on the broadcast deal to cover 2017–2022. In the new deal, a total of 30 games would be shown each season on ESPN networks. Each team would receive around $1 million per year.[[311]](#cite_note-334)

On April 22, 2019, [CBS Sports Network](/wiki/CBS_Sports_Network "CBS Sports Network") reached a multi-year deal to televise 40 regular-season weekend and primetime WNBA games, beginning in the [2019 season](/wiki/2019_WNBA_season "2019 WNBA season").[[312]](#cite_note-335)[[313]](#cite_note-336)

On April 20, 2023, Ion Television signed a multi-year deal with the WNBA to air a 15-week slate of [doubleheader](/wiki/Doubleheader_(television) "Doubleheader (television)") games on Friday nights (branded as *WNBA Friday Night Spotlight on ION*), beginning with that year's [regular season](/wiki/2023_WNBA_season "2023 WNBA season"). It will be the first national sports broadcast carried by Ion since 2011, and marks the first ever television contract for [Scripps Sports](/wiki/Scripps_Sports "Scripps Sports"), which was founded by the [E. W. Scripps Company](/wiki/E._W._Scripps_Company "E. W. Scripps Company") in December 2022 to acquire sports events for Ion and the group's local television stations. The agreement also grants local rights to selected Ion [O&Os](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_stations "Owned-and-operated stations") for games involving regional WNBA teams, which stations may carry in early- or late-evening broadcast windows depending on tip-off time.[[314]](#cite_note-337)[[315]](#cite_note-338)[[316]](#cite_note-339) The first broadcast was on May 26, 2023, when the [Washington Mystics](/wiki/Washington_Mystics "Washington Mystics") visited the [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky").[[317]](#cite_note-340) On June 13, 2025, the WNBA announced that they have extended their agreement with Scripps Sports to continue their Friday Night Showcase package of games on Ion Television.[[318]](#cite_note-341) On September 30, 2025, the WNBA announced that it has also entered an 11-year agreement with [Versant](/wiki/Versant "Versant"), the cable spin-off company of [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") owned by [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") shareholders, where its games will air on [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network").[[319]](#cite_note-342)[[320]](#cite_note-343)

Starting in 2026, the WNBA will begin a new 11-year agreement that will see games return to NBC Sports, with new airings on USA Sports, as well as remaining on ESPN/ABC and Amazon Prime Video. With this new deal, the league will distribute more than 125 games nationally (a minimum of 25 across ESPN/ABC, 50 across NBC platforms, and 30 on Prime Video) and worldwide. ESPN continues on as the league's All-Star and Draft broadcaster, Prime Video continues as the broadcaster for the league's Commissioner's Cup games, and NBC will pick up rights to all USA Basketball games. In addition, the WNBA Playoffs and Finals will see a wider distribution across the three partners, where each partner airs a first round series annually (two on ESPN/ABC and one each on NBC platforms and Prime Video), and rotates turns airing the semifinals and Finals (eight semifinals and five Finals on ESPN/ABC and seven semifinals and three Finals on NBC platforms and Prime Video). The WNBA also stated that they are expected to add additional media partners prior to the first year of the new deal. On March 25, 2026, the WNBA announced that the league reached a new long-term agreement with [CBS Sports](/wiki/CBS_Sports "CBS Sports") to broadcast 20 regular season games on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") and [Paramount+](/wiki/Paramount%2B "Paramount+").[[321]](#cite_note-344)

Some teams offer games on local radio, while all teams have some games broadcast on local television stations:

* **[Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta_Dream "Atlanta Dream")** – [WPCH-TV](/wiki/WPCH-TV "WPCH-TV")
* **[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky")** – [WCIU-TV](/wiki/WCIU-TV "WCIU-TV")
* **[Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun")** – [NBC Sports Boston](/wiki/NBC_Sports_Boston "NBC Sports Boston"), [New England Cable News](/wiki/New_England_Cable_News "New England Cable News"), [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)")
* **[Dallas](/wiki/Dallas_Wings "Dallas Wings")** – [WFAA](/wiki/WFAA "WFAA"), [KFAA-TV](/wiki/KFAA-TV "KFAA-TV")[[322]](#cite_note-345)
* **[Indiana](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever")** – [WTHR](/wiki/WTHR "WTHR"), [WALV](/wiki/WALV-CD "WALV-CD")

### WNBA League Pass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: WNBA League Pass")]

In 2009, the WNBA announced the launch of *WNBA LiveAccess*, a feature on WNBA.com that provides fans with access to more than 200 live game webcasts throughout the WNBA season. All of the *WNBA LiveAccess* games are then archived for on-demand viewing. Most games (except broadcasts on [ABC, ESPN or ESPN2](/wiki/WNBA_on_ESPN "WNBA on ESPN"), which are available on [ESPN3](/wiki/ESPN3 "ESPN3")) are available via this system. The first use of LiveAccess was the E League versus [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky") preseason game.[[323]](#cite_note-346)

Before the 2011 season, LiveAccess was given an overhaul, and the system became more reliable and many new features were added. Before the 2012 season, it was announced that users of LiveAccess would have to pay a $4.99 subscription fee to use the service. In 2013, this was increased to $14.99. In 2014 the streaming service was renamed [WNBA League Pass](/wiki/WNBA_League_Pass "WNBA League Pass").

WNBA League Pass is available as part of the WNBA App, the free mobile application available on the iPhone, iPad and Android devices and costs US$16.99 for the season. Games airing on ESPN, ESPN2 as well as other games taking place during the telecast windows of ESPN and ESPN2 games, are not available live on WNBA League Pass. However, those games will be available on-demand shortly after the conclusion of their live broadcast.[[324]](#cite_note-347)

## Offseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Offseason")]

In July 2023, [Napheesa Collier](/wiki/Napheesa_Collier "Napheesa Collier") and [Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart") announced [Unrivaled](/wiki/Unrivaled_(basketball_league) "Unrivaled (basketball league)"), a women's professional three-on-three basketball league.[[335]](#cite_note-358) The league was founded, in part, to allow WNBA players to play domestically and to bypass complications from the WNBA's prioritization rule for players who choose to play overseas in the WNBA offseason. Unrivaled's inaugural season, consisting of six teams of six players debuted in January 2025 in the [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami")-area community of [Medley, Florida](/wiki/Medley,_Florida "Medley, Florida").[[336]](#cite_note-Yahoo-359)
